title: Title: Global Cross-Resource Search with Keyboard Navigation · apache/superset · Discussion #39652 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Problem: Superset today has separate search bars inside each list view — one for Charts, one for Dashboards, one for Datasets, and one for SQL Lab saved queries. If a user remembers the name of something but not where it lives, they must check each section manually. There is no single entry point to find anything across the entire platform. For large organizations with hundreds of dashboards and thousands of charts this becomes a serious productivity problem. Proposed Solution: Add a persistent global search bar to the Superset top navigation, triggerable from anywhere in the application using the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+K on Windows or Cmd+K on Mac — the same convention used by Notion, Linear, Figma, and VS Code. Typing in the bar searches across five resource types simultaneously: Dashboards, Charts, Datasets, SQL Lab saved queries, and Database connections. Results appear instantly in a dropdown grouped by resource type. Each result shows the resource name, its type icon, and who last modified it. Arrow keys navigate between results, Enter opens the selected resource, and Escape closes the panel. The search indexes resource names, descriptions, and owner names. Results are filtered by the current user's access permissions so users only see resources they are allowed to open. What Changes: Backend → new /api/v1/search?q= endpoint that queries all resource tables in parallel Backend → permission filtering applied per resource type in the same query Frontend → global search input in the top nav bar (always visible) Frontend → keyboard shortcut listener (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) that focuses the input Frontend → dropdown results panel with grouped sections and keyboard navigation Frontend → recently viewed resources shown when the input is focused but empty Why It Is Not Implemented Yet: A cross-resource search requires a unified backend endpoint that queries multiple unrelated tables and applies different permission checks per resource type. The current architecture handles each resource type in a completely separate API with no shared search layer. ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ SIP-007 Title: Collaborative In-Dashboard Chart Comments with Threading Problem: Superset dashboards are shared across teams — finance, product, operations — but the platform offers no way for viewers to communicate about the data they are looking at. When someone spots an unusual number on a chart, their only option is to take a screenshot and paste it into Slack or an email, losing all context. There is no in-app way to ask a question about a specific chart, flag a data issue, or leave a note for the next person who opens the dashboard. This makes Superset purely a consumption tool with no collaboration layer. Proposed Solution: Add a comment system anchored to individual charts on a dashboard. Any user with at least Viewer access to a dashboard can open a comment panel for any chart by clicking a speech bubble icon that appears on hover. The panel shows existing comments in
author: iskanderknani2005-oss

title: Title: Chart Version History and Rollback · apache/superset · Discussion #39651 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Problem: Every time a chart is saved in Superset, the previous configuration is permanently overwritten. If a data analyst spends two hours building a chart, then a colleague edits it and saves incorrectly, the original work is gone forever. There is no undo history, no changelog, no way to see what changed between saves, and no way to go back. This is one of the most common sources of frustration reported by teams sharing dashboards. Proposed Solution: Every time a chart is saved, Superset stores a lightweight snapshot of the chart's full JSON configuration in a new chart_versions table. Each snapshot records the chart ID, the timestamp of the save, the user who saved it, and a JSON diff showing exactly what changed from the previous version. Users access this through a History button in the chart editor toolbar. Clicking it opens a side panel listing all previous versions in reverse chronological order. Each entry shows the timestamp, the author name, and a summary of what changed such as "changed chart type from Bar to Line" or "updated metric from COUNT to SUM". Clicking any version shows a visual side-by-side diff. Clicking Restore replaces the current configuration with that version and saves a new snapshot so the restore action itself is also recorded. What Changes: Database → new chart_versions table with columns: id, chart_id, user_id, created_at, config_snapshot (JSON), diff_summary (text) Backend → new /api/v1/charts/{id}/versions endpoint (GET list, POST restore) Backend → hook into existing chart save flow to auto-snapshot on every PUT Frontend → History button in chart editor toolbar Frontend → version list side panel with diff viewer Frontend → Restore button per version entry Storage Impact: JSON diffs are typically under 2KB per save. A chart saved 100 times uses roughly 200KB. A configurable MAX_CHART_VERSION_HISTORY setting caps how many snapshots are kept per chart to control database growth. Why It Is Not Implemented Yet: No snapshot mechanism exists in the current chart save flow. Adding it requires a database migration, a new API endpoint, and a diff rendering component on the frontend — three separate pieces that need to be built together.
author: iskanderknani2005-oss

title: Title: Query Cost Estimator in SQL Lab Before Execution · apache/superset · Discussion #39650 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Problem: Right now when a user types a query in SQL Lab and clicks Run, Superset fires the full query against the database immediately. There is zero warning if that query will scan 500 million rows, join four unindexed tables, or cost $40 on a cloud warehouse like BigQuery or Snowflake. Junior analysts frequently cause accidental full table scans that slow down the entire database for other users. There is no way to know the damage before it happens. Proposed Solution: Add a second button next to Run called "Estimate Cost". When clicked, Superset sends the query to the database using its native dry-run or EXPLAIN mechanism instead of actually executing it. The result is displayed as a banner directly below the SQL editor showing three values: estimated rows scanned, estimated data volume in MB or GB, and estimated cost in credits or dollars where the database supports it. The user then decides whether to run the query or refine it first. How It Works Per Database: BigQuery → uses jobs.query with dryRun: true flag Snowflake → uses EXPLAIN command which returns credit estimate Trino → uses EXPLAIN ANALYZE with VERBOSE option Presto → uses EXPLAIN with TYPE DISTRIBUTED PostgreSQL → uses EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON) for cost nodes Other → falls back to a row count estimate via SELECT COUNT(*) What Changes: Backend → new /api/v1/sqllab/estimate endpoint per engine Frontend → new Estimate Cost button in SQL Lab toolbar Frontend → cost banner component below the editor Config → feature flag ENABLE_QUERY_COST_ESTIMATE to toggle per deployment Why It Is Not Implemented Yet: Each database exposes cost estimation differently. A unified abstraction layer needs to be built in the DB engine spec so each connector can register its own estimate method. This is the main missing piece.
author: iskanderknani2005-oss

title: Idea: Git-graph-inspired visual change history in the Change sidebar · apache/superset · Discussion #39648 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: I’d like to propose a visual change history experience for Superset, inspired by VS Code’s Git Graph, integrated into the existing Change sidebar. Problem For dashboards (and potentially charts later), linear change history is often not enough to understand how an object evolved over time, especially when multiple edits happen across iterations. Users may want to quickly answer questions like: what changed recently? which version introduced a regression? how does the current version relate to an earlier saved state? can I visually compare revisions before restoring? Proposal Add an optional graph view to the existing Change sidebar. This view would: visualize saved revisions as nodes show parent/merge relationships as edges let users select a node to inspect metadata (author, timestamp, summary) support compare-to-current / compare-to-previous support restoring a selected revision Why in the Change sidebar? The Change sidebar is already the natural place where users expect version/change-related actions. A graph view there would extend the current workflow instead of introducing a separate history page. Initial scope To keep this narrow, I suggest starting with: dashboard-only support read-only visual history compare revisions restore a previous revision Non-goals for an initial version: full Git semantics arbitrary branching operations real-time merge conflict resolution support for every Superset object type Open questions Does the current revision model already expose enough lineage metadata for a graph? Would maintainers prefer this as a core feature, a design proposal, or an extension candidate? Is the Change sidebar considered the right entry point for this workflow? If this direction makes sense, I’m happy to turn it into a SIP with a more detailed UX and implementation proposal.

title: Customize dark mode colors · apache/superset · Discussion #39564 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Good morning, is it possible to customize the colors of the dark mode across the entire interface from the config.py file?
author: Yisel-Hernandez
comment_excerpt: Yes, you can customize dark mode colors from your superset_config.py using the THEME setting. Superset uses Ant Design v5's token-based theming system [1] . To enable dark mode with custom colors, add something like this: THEME = { "algorithm" : "dark" , "token" : { "colorPrimary" : "#2893B3" , "colorError" : "#e04355" , "colorWarning" : "#fcc700" , "colorSuccess" : "#5ac189" , "colorInfo" : "#66bcfe" , } } The "algorithm": "dark" part activates dark mode, and the token dictionary lets you override specific colors using Ant Design v5 token names [2] . You can see the default token values in the Theme.tsx file and example dark themes in exampleThemes.ts . A couple of useful feature flags you can also enable in your config [3] : THEME_ENABLE_DARK_THEME_SWITCH — adds a toggle in the navbar to || @dosu I’d like to change, for example, the background color. Right now it seems to be black, but I want to modify it. How can I identify the specific class or identifier for the background color? And how can I enable and use THEME_ALLOW_THEME_EDITOR_BETA? || @Yisel-Hernandez The dark mode background is controlled by Ant Design v5 tokens . When you set "algorithm": "dark" , Ant Design's dark algorithm auto-generates background colors from seed values. The main tokens you'll want to override are: colorBgBase — the base page background (the "black" you're seeing) [1] colorBgContainer — the container/content area background [2] colorBgLayout — the layout/sidebar background [3] To change the background to a dark gray instead of black, for example: THEME = { "algorithm" : "dark" , "token" : { "colorBgBase" : "#1a1a2e" , "colorBgContainer" : "#16213e" , "colorBgLayout" : "#0f3460" , } } To enable the theme editor , add this to your superset_config.py [4] : DEFAULT_FEATURE_FLAGS = { "THEME_ALLOW_THEME_EDITOR_BETA" : True , } Once enabled, a palette ic || @dosu From the dark mode templates, I changed these colors and this is the code it provided: { "token": { "colorPrimary": "#0f172a", "colorInfo": "#0f172a", "colorBgBase": "#0f172a", "colorLink": "#1677ff", "colorSuccess": "#10b981", "colorError": "#dc0000", "colorLinkHover": "#ffffff", "colorLinkActive": "#ffffff" }, "algorithm": "dark" } How can I set this in the config.py so the colors remain fixed and not just for the current session? I tried this and it didn’t work: THEME = { "algorithm": "dark", "token": { "colorBgBase": "#1a1a2e", "colorBgContainer": "#16213e", "colorBgLayout": "#0f3460", } } || @Yisel-Hernandez The most likely reason your THEME config didn't work is that browser localStorage is overriding your server-side settings . When you used the theme editor, it saved customizations to localStorage (key superset-theme ), and those take priority over the config in superset_config.py [1] . Here's what you need to do: 1. Set your theme in superset_config.py (not config.py directly — use your override file): THEME = { "algorithm" : "dark" , "token" : { "colorPrimary" : "#0f172a" , "colorInfo" : "#0f172a" , "colorBgBase" : "#0f172a" , "colorLink" : "#1677ff" , "colorSuccess" : "#10b981" , "colorError" : "#dc0000" , "colorLinkHover" : "#ffffff" , "colorLinkActive" : "#ffffff" } } 2. Clear browser localStorage — this is the critical step most people miss: Open browser DevTools (F12 || @dosu I did it exactly as you said and added it to superset_config.py, but it doesn’t work. It does not apply the color customization permanently. I’m attaching a screenshot of how I did it, and I also tried placing it in THEME_DEFAULT, but that didn’t work either. Should I keep anything else in mind, or do you know what might be happening? (I also cleared the cache.)

author: lilotter45
comment_excerpt: This behavior isn't controlled by a specific permission — it's hardcoded role-based logic in the populate_owner_list() function in superset/commands/utils.py . The key line is: if g . user . id not in owner_ids and not security_manager . is_admin (): owner_ids . append ( g . user . id ) This means non-admin users (including Alpha) are always auto-added as co-owners when they assign ownership, while Admin users bypass this because security_manager.is_admin() returns True for them [1] . The is_admin() check simply looks for the role matching the AUTH_ROLE_ADMIN config value (defaults to "Admin" ) [2] . So unfortunately, there's no permission you can grant to the Alpha role to get the same behavior — it's a hardcoded Admin-only bypass. To give Alpha users this ability, you'd need to either mo

title: New report type: webhook · apache/superset · Discussion #29928 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I made some upgrade on the Superset: I add webhook as selectable report type. By the use of webhook the user can post csv/png/pdf to any endpoint, with some informative parameters as: name of the report id of the report report type (chart, or dashboard) content id content format For security reasons I made a new optional parameter: WEBHOOK_SECRET. It's a secret string between the Superset and the webhook endpoint. In the case where WEBHOOK_SECRET is filled, Superset adds an "X-Webhook-Signature" parameter to the header of the post call, which hashes the json data to be sent. The receiving party can verify that the party sending the webhook is the real sender by hashing the received json data and comparing it with the sending party's "X-Webhook-Signature" parameter. If the two parameters do not match, the receiving party may reject the call because the sender is not the supposed host. On the report log, we can get feedback about the successful, and not successful requests as well. I'm happy to share my code with you guys!

title: How to edit css dashboard? · apache/superset · Discussion #34788 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: In Superset 5.0.0, How to edit css dashboard so there is no grid when try to edit dashboard? @dosu
author: johannes-ws
comment_excerpt: In Superset 5.0.0, the grid overlay you see when editing a dashboard is rendered only in edit mode and mainly appears during component resizing. It's controlled by CSS classes like .grid-column-guide and .empty-droptarget. You can hide these overlays by injecting custom CSS into your dashboard. To do this, open your dashboard, click the "Edit dashboard" button, then open the "Properties" panel (usually via the ⚙️ or "..." menu), and look for the "CSS" or "Custom CSS" section. Paste the following CSS to hide the grid overlays: . grid-column-guide , . empty-droptarget { display : none !important ; } This will hide the grid lines and drop targets while editing. If you want to target only the guides that appear during resizing, you can use just .grid-column-guide { display: none !important; }  || Every i open edit dashboard menu, why there is error message 'An error occurred while fetching available CSS templates' however i access this as admin? @dosu || That error means the dashboard editor's frontend tried to fetch CSS templates from the backend API endpoint /api/v1/css_template/ , but the request failed. For admin users, this usually points to one of these root causes: the css_templates table is missing or corrupted in your database, permissions are out of sync (even admins can hit this if roles weren't synced after an upgrade), or the backend API isn't registered or is failing due to a server/database issue. There’s no feature flag or config toggle for this—the main dependencies are the database schema and permissions. To troubleshoot: check your Superset server logs for errors when you open the dashboard editor, verify the css_templates table exists in your database, and run superset sync_roles to repair permissions. You can also try  || why i don't see the Edit CSS when i choose Edit Dashboard || why i don't see the Edit CSS when i choose Edit Dashboard This is the same issue I am facing. Anyone have an idea what to do to enable this "Edit CSS" option on the dashboard edit option? || why i don't see the Edit CSS when i choose Edit Dashboard This is the same issue I am facing. title: New “Search by” option · apache/superset · Discussion #39565 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: How can I hide or disable the new “Search by” functionality in table charts from the config.py file? It’s causing searches to not work correctly—it only searches based on the first part of the text in the data.
author: Yisel-Hernandez
comment_excerpt: @Yisel-Hernandez There's currently no config.py setting or feature flag to globally disable the "Search by" functionality across all table charts [1] . However, you can control it at the chart level . The "Search by" dropdown only appears when both server-side pagination and the search box are enabled [2] . So to hide it, you can either: Disable Server pagination in the chart configuration, or Disable the Search box option Regarding the search behavior you described — it only matching the first part of text — this is intentional when server-side pagination is enabled. The search uses a prefix match ( ILIKE 'searchText%' ), so only values starting with your search term are returned [3] . For example, searching "Sound" won't match "Artist/Soundtrack". title: How to connect to databases via Oauth2 using user's token? · apache/superset · Discussion #39558 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hello, We are trying to connect to Trino from Superset using Oauth2 authentification and i wan't to use the token from the connected user to Trino. Is it possible ? How to achieve it ?
author: datasc24
comment_excerpt: Yes, Superset supports OAuth2 authentication for Trino database connections, and there are two approaches to pass a user's token to Trino: Approach 1: Per-Database OAuth2 (available now) Superset has built-in per-user, per-database OAuth2 token management. You configure OAuth2 clients in superset_config.py [1] : DATABASE_OAUTH2_CLIENTS = { "trino" : { "client_id" : "..." , "client_secret" : "..." , "authorization_uri" : "..." , "token_uri" : "..." , "scope" : "..." } } With this setup, users go through an OAuth2 dance when they first query Trino. Superset stores the token (encrypted) per user and automatically refreshes it when expired [2] . The Trino engine spec passes the token as a Bearer header via a custom HTTP session [3] . Approach 2: Upstream OAuth Token Forwarding (reuse Superset

title: Docker image build using slim-trixie · apache/superset · Discussion #39526 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I see on the Dockerfile that the default version used to build the image is based on trixie distro, but all the images published on DockerHub are based on bookworm . Our security scan is showing many CVE due to bookworm usage, are you planning to publish a tag based on trixie distro? Thanks, Antonio
author: antonioT90
comment_excerpt: Hi @antonioT90 ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. The Dockerfile on the master branch has already been migrated from Bookworm to Debian 13 (Trixie) — it now uses python:3.11.13-slim-trixie and node:20-trixie-slim as base images [1] . This was done in PR #34821 , specifically to pick up updated security patches and mitigations. A follow-up PR #36047 further bumped to Python 3.11.14-slim-trixie. If you're still seeing Bookworm-based images on DockerHub, you're likely pulling a tag tied to an older release (e.g., 4.x). The newer builds from master (and future releases built from it) should all be Trixie-based. You can verify by pulling the master or latest tag and checking with docker run --rm apache/superset:latest cat /etc/os-release . To reply, just mention @dosu . I see that you are setting PY_VER=3.10-slim-bookworm || Good catch. The override is coming from the supersetbot tool's docker.js module , which is what the GitHub Actions workflows use to build images. It hardcodes BASE_PY_IMAGE = '3.10-slim-bookworm' and passes it as --build-arg PY_VER=3.10-slim-bookworm to the Docker build, overriding whatever default is in the Dockerfile [1] . So even though PR #34821 merged the Trixie change into the Dockerfile, the supersetbot tool still forces Bookworm for all published image presets ( dev , lean , py310 , py311 , websocket , dockerize , ci ). The preset-to-version mapping in supersetbot currently looks like: dev , lean , websocket , ci , dockerize → 3.10-slim-bookworm py310 → 3.10-slim-bookworm py311 → 3.11-slim-bookworm py312 → 3.12-slim-bookworm All Bookworm. So the Trixie migration would also need a c || Do you plan to make this change?

author: serverdevil
comment_excerpt: The 'odelasticsearch' dialect is commented out in Superset's dialect mapping, so Superset defaults to a generic SQL dialect for query parsing and generation. This fallback uses backticks for quoting identifiers (e.g., FROM \ default`.logs`), which Opensearch SQL does not support, leading to parsing errors when creating datasets or running internal queries [source] . Currently, there is no built-in workaround in Superset for this issue. The SQL_QUERY_MUTATOR config can alter outgoing queries, but it does not reliably affect internal queries like those used for dataset metadata [source] . Superset does allow for extending SQL dialects via SQLGLOT_DIALECTS_EXTENSIONS , but this only works if the dialect is referenced in the code, which is not the case for 'odelasticsearch' right now. To resol || @dosu Can you outline detailed contributions required to support a new dialect for opensearch in superset? || To add Opensearch dialect support in Superset, you’ll need to: Implement or extend an EngineSpec subclass for Opensearch in superset/db_engine_specs/ . Use classes like PrestoEngineSpec or PostgresEngineSpec as templates—define engine name, aliases, driver mappings, column type mappings, and SQL generation logic. Pay special attention to identifier quoting and SQL syntax that Opensearch expects [ example ]. Ensure there is a corresponding sqlglot dialect for Opensearch. If one doesn’t exist, you may need to contribute it upstream to sqlglot or implement it locally. This is crucial for correct SQL parsing and formatting, especially for identifier quoting (avoid backticks, use double quotes or none as required by Opensearch) [ reference ]. Register the new dialect in the SQLGLOT_DIALECTS map || opensearch 's sql rest api states that opensearch supports delimited identifiers using backticks. Also the query above when made directly to opensearch using url like below works fine curl -XPOST http://localhost:9200/_plugins/_sql -u 'admin:admin' -k -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"query": "SELECT * FROM `default`.logs LIMIT 50"}' { "schema": [ { "name": "log", "type": "text" }, { "name": "@timestamp", "type": "timestamp" } ], "datarows": [ [ "2025-07-04T10:15:01.057694+00:00 ip-172-31-8-180 CRON[1581970]: pam_unix(cron:session): session opened for user root(uid=0) by root(uid=0)", "2025-07-04 10:15:01.058" ], [ "2025-07-04T10:15:01.066223+00:00 ip-172-31-8-180 CRON[1581970]: pam_unix(cron:session): session closed for user root", "2025-07-04 10:15:01.066" ], ... } So the questio || Superset fails to parse queries with backticks for Opensearch because its internal SQL parser (sqlglot) doesn't know how to handle Opensearch-specific syntax. The core issue is that Superset does not map the 'odelasticsearch' engine to any sqlglot dialect in its SQLGLOT_DIALECTS config—it's commented out in the code. As a result, Superset defaults to a generic dialect that doesn't recognize backtick-quoted identifiers, causing the parsing error before the query ever reaches Opensearch, even though Opensearch itself supports and expects backticks for delimited identifiers. This failure happens entirely inside Superset's parsing step, not in query construction or in Opensearch itself. To fix this, Superset would need a dialect mapping for 'odelasticsearch' that supports backtick quoting, eit || I tried implementing a bare minimum dialect but any custom stuff that I try to do and build a docker image with --target dev , the database connection modal's "Connect" button never gets enabled. I even reverted all my changes and deployed a pristine master branch as a docker image, the same issue persists. Unable to figure out why building your own image results in "Connect" not being enabled, even for regular sources like Postgres using the SQLAlchemy URI option. || @serverdevil in case you're still stuck here, I'm working on an OpenSearch dialect that could help fixing this issue: #39538

title: Best practice for deleting users in Superset in regards to GDPR · apache/superset · Discussion #39407 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I am running Superset and I am currently looking into how to delete users which has the right to be forgotten according to GDPR. I’m trying to understand what the recommended / best practice is for this scenario: Has anyone implemented an automated cleanup process? Any guidance, references to documentation, or real world experience would be greatly appreciated. Thanks!
author: SkaziBavaria
comment_excerpt: I would separate the legal policy from the technical action here. This is not legal advice, but technically I would avoid a blind hard delete until you have mapped the references that matter in your deployment. Superset users are Flask-AppBuilder users, and Superset metadata can reference users for ownership, created/changed metadata, audit/event logs, reports, saved queries, dashboards, charts, and related objects. A hard delete may be possible in a narrow setup, but it can also break history or ownership unless those references are reassigned or anonymized first. A safer automation usually looks like: disable the account and revoke access immediately reassign owned content to a service/admin account where needed anonymize personal fields such as username, first/last name, email, avatar,

title: watermark adding · apache/superset · Discussion #39327 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: i need to add a watermark when i download charts/tables as images
author: srijani-smsrc
comment_excerpt: I do not see a built-in watermark option on the current image download path. For dashboards, Superset generates a cached screenshot and then serves it as PNG or PDF through the screenshot endpoint. The API exposes the download format, but not a watermark parameter or configuration hook. So this is custom work. The least invasive approaches are: add a CSS/DOM watermark overlay that is visible in the screenshot context customize the frontend download flow for your deployment post-process the PNG/PDF bytes after screenshot generation If you need this for compliance, I would prefer the post-processing route or a clearly scoped custom screenshot task, because it makes the watermark independent of dashboard authors accidentally deleting or hiding an overlay.

author: HaruspexSan
comment_excerpt: I would base this on Superset's import/export bundle flow, not on editing exported files with sed . A practical GitOps shape is: superset export_dashboards -f dashboards.zip superset export_datasources -f datasources.zip Commit those bundles, or commit the unpacked YAML if your workflow validates the bundle structure. In Kubernetes, run the corresponding import from an init job or release job after migrations and before users start relying on the dashboards: superset import_datasources -p /path/datasources.zip -u admin superset import_dashboards -p /path/dashboards.zip -u admin The key is to make environment-specific things stable outside the dashboard JSON/YAML: database connections, secrets, SQLAlchemy URIs, and ownership. author: hallebanis
comment_excerpt: Hi @hallebanis ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. @hallebanis This is a frequently reported issue — the Dataset API returns empty results because Bearer tokens alone don't establish the session cookies that Superset's authorization filters require. The token authenticates you (hence the 200 OK), but the DatasourceFilter can't resolve your permissions without a proper session, so it silently returns zero results instead of an error [1] [2] . The fix is to use a requests.Session() and call the CSRF token endpoint with your Authorization header before making API calls. This establishes the session cookies: import requests session = requests . Session () # 1. Login login_resp = session . post ( "http://localhost:8088/api/v1/security/login" , json = { "username" : "admin" , "password

author: sreekanthpamujula9-byte
comment_excerpt: Hey @sreekanthpamujula9-byte — here's a breakdown of how to approach this migration: Migrating Your Metadata & Assets The most complete approach is to back up your entire metadata database (PostgreSQL/MySQL) using native tools like pg_dump or mysqldump and restore it on your new server [1] . This captures everything — dashboards, charts, datasets, database connections, users, roles, and permissions. Alternatively, Superset provides CLI export/import commands [2] : superset export_dashboards / superset import_dashboards superset export_datasources / superset import_datasources However, these do not include users, roles, or permissions , and database passwords are never exported for security reasons — you'll need to re-enter them manually on the new server [3] . User IDs also aren't automati

author: abhinavty123
comment_excerpt: Hi @abhinavty123 ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. You can back up all your Superset data, but there are a few things to know. Superset stores everything—including charts, dashboards, datasets, users, roles, and permissions—in its metadata database (like PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite). The only way to get a full backup (including users, roles, and permissions) is to back up this entire metadata database using your database's backup tools (for example, pg_dump for PostgreSQL or mysqldump for MySQL) source . Superset also provides CLI commands to export/import dashboards and datasets as ZIP files ( superset export_dashboards , superset export_datasources , superset import_dashboards , superset import_datasources ). author: ZeroWiggliness
comment_excerpt: Hi @ZeroWiggliness ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. Yes, frontend test flakiness (especially timeouts) is a well-documented problem in Superset. The team has been actively fixing these across multiple PRs. Here's what's going on and what you can try: Root cause for most timeouts: The most common culprit is unmocked API endpoints — when a component makes an API call on mount but the test doesn't mock that endpoint, Jest hangs waiting for a response until it hits the 20-second timeout [1] . The global timeout is set to 20000ms in jest.config.js [2] . Specifically for EmbeddedModal.test.tsx : There's a race condition in the test setup where resetMockApi() is called after render() , but the component's useEffect fires during render — meaning API calls can execute before mocks are  || Flaky timeout failures in CI are really common in large frontend test suites — you're definitely not alone. The root cause is usually one of two things: runner resource contention (slower runner = more timeouts) or test isolation issues where shared state makes tests order-dependent. One thing that helps is analyzing your CI history to see which specific tests are flaky vs. consistently slow. If a test fails 30% of runs but passes on retry, that's a different problem than a test that consistently times out. I built Kleore specifically to surface this — it connects to GitHub, analyzes your CI run history, and ranks tests by failure rate and cost. Free tier shows you the full picture. Might help identify which tests are genuinely flaky vs. just slow.

comment_excerpt: Hi @yugviren ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. The 403 errors you're seeing on dashboard, dataset, and chart API calls are almost certainly a permissions issue on the guest role , not a missing bearer token. The Embedded SDK handles authentication automatically — you do not need to pass a bearer token in your JS code. The guest token (sent via the X-GuestToken header) covers all sub-requests (dashboards, charts, datasets) [1] . Here's a checklist to resolve the 403s: Enable the feature flag in superset_config.py [2] : FEATURE_FLAGS = { "EMBEDDED_SUPERSET" : True } Ensure the guest role has sufficient permissions. The role used is whatever GUEST_ROLE_NAME is set to (default: "Public" ). It needs at minimum [3] : can read on Dashboard, Chart, Dataset, Database can list , can get  || @ChadRosseau , Thanks For considering my Problem! I had try everything you had mention but still last 3 API facing 403. || Need to add custom security code within config file to remove 403 error........... class CustomSecurityManager(SupersetSecurityManager): def has_access(self, permission_name, view_name): user = current_user print(f"[AUTH CHECK] user={user} permission={permission_name} view={view_name}") # Allow guest token users if user and getattr(user, "is_guest_user", False): return True return super().has_access(permission_name, view_name) CUSTOM_SECURITY_MANAGER = CustomSecurityManager

title: Add support for per-database machine OAuth2 credentials for Databricks (SQL Endpoint) · apache/superset · Discussion #39405 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature request The Databricks SQL Connector endpoint officially supports using an OAuth Service Principal for integrations ("machine-to-machine"), see docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/databricks/dev-tools/python-sql-connector#auth-m2m (same for GCP and AWS). For my use case, I want to set up a service principal to be used by a single database, independent of users. This would allow me to set up multiple sources using different OAuth2 credentials, and avoid having to rely on Databaricks PATs or other workarounds that require a "raw" token embedded in the SQLAlchemy URI. Note Supporting this use case would require installing the databricks-sdk library, but given that this is already a requirement for each engine type, I don't see this as a blocker. The databricks engine can still be used without it, if the end-user does not wish to use M2M authentication. Implementation suggestions In the current implementation, it would make sense to make use of the database "extra" arguments, adding in two additional fields ( client_id and client_secret or something similar), which should be encrypted/masked (similar to how the token is handled currently). The host is already included in the sqlalchemy URI. However, the current workflow to create the SQLAlchemy engine only allows for raw data, see flow in https://github.com/apache/superset/blob/master/superset/models/core.py#L479 . While there is a hook via DBEngineSpec.adjust_engine_params , it only includes current "connect_args". My suggestion would be to add a new hook to the engine specs that would allow it to "compute" additional connect args. The default would do nothing ( return {} ), and the databricks implementation would check if the required fields were set and use that to create the credential_provider (see docs example). As I am not familiar with the full database/engine spec and integration, please add in any other parts that would be required to make this happen. I would be happy to make a PR with a first implementation to make this possible.
author: cpnielsen

title: Missing option to select number of rows per page · apache/superset · Discussion #39397 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi everyone, Since upgrading to Superset 6.0, I noticed that in the new Table chart it is no longer possible for end users to select the number of rows displayed per page, as was possible in the previous version. Instead, there is now a “search” field and server-side pagination. Is there any way to re-enable or configure the option for users to choose how many rows to display per page? In some use cases, this was a very useful feature for navigation and data exploration, and its absence has impacted the user experience. If this was an intentional change, is there any recommended workaround or best practice to achieve similar behavior? Thanks in advance!
author: Yisel-Hernandez
comment_excerpt: The "rows per page" selector wasn't actually removed in Superset 6.0 — it's conditionally hidden when server-side pagination is enabled on the chart. When server pagination is active, you see the search field and server pagination controls instead of the traditional page length dropdown. To restore the traditional "rows per page" dropdown: Edit your Table chart In the chart configuration panel, find and uncheck the "Server pagination" option [1] The page_length control will reappear with options like 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 rows per page [2] Save the chart When server pagination is disabled , the client-side "rows per page" selector is visible to end users as before. Trade-off to be aware of: Client-side pagination has a max row limit of 100K rows ( SQL_MAX_ROW ), while server-side pagination || Thanks for the follow-up! This is likely caused by how the page_length setting interacts with Superset's auto-pagination logic. When page_length is left at its default (not explicitly set), Superset auto-calculates whether to paginate based on your dataset size [1] : If rows × columns > 5000 → paginate at 200 rows/page (dropdown appears) If rows × columns ≤ 5000 → no pagination, pageSize = 0 (dropdown is hidden ) The page size selector is only rendered when initialPageSize > 0 [2] . So for smaller datasets, the dropdown won't appear at all unless you explicitly configure it. To fix this , in the chart editor (Explore view): Look for the "Page Length" control in the Data panel (it should be visible now that server pagination is off) Explicitly set it to a value like 10 , 20 , or 50 instead  || @dosu In my case, the "page length" control only appears when I enable server pagination. However, when I disable server pagination, the rows-per-page dropdown does not appear at all. So currently, the dropdown is never available in the expected way. Also, the dataset contains a large number of records, so pagination should be applicable. In this situation, is there anything else I should check or configure? Or could this be a bug? || That's an interesting symptom. I dug into the source code, and the visibility logic for these controls is actually correct — page_length is configured to show when server pagination is off , and server_page_length is configured to show when server pagination is on [1] [2] . So what you're describing shouldn't happen based on the current code. A few things worth checking: Are these the same control? When server pagination is enabled, the control that appears is actually called "Server Page Length" , not "Page Length." They look very similar but are separate controls. When you toggle server pagination off, "Server Page Length" disappears and "Page Length" should appear in its place — but it may render in a slightly different position in the control panel, so it could be easy to miss. Try a h || @dosu I am currently using Apache Superset 6.0.0. I’m attaching a screenshot of the Data configuration panel for the chart. As you can see, I only have the "Server page length" option available, and not a general "Page length" option as you mentioned. Additionally, when I disable server pagination, all pagination controls disappear entirely, and the rows-per-page dropdown does not show up. Could you please clarify if this is expected behavior in this version or if there is something else I should check? || Thanks for the screenshot and details — this helps narrow it down. The page_length control does exist in the code and its visibility logic is correct (it should appear when server pagination is off) [1] . However, there's a key detail: page_length and server_page_length are in different sections of the control panel: "Server Page Length" is in the Query section (near the top) "Page Length" is in the Options section (further down) Version 6.0.0 has a known scrolling bug in the Explore control panel that can prevent you from scrolling down to the Options section where page_length lives. Several fixes for this were merged in April 2026 [2] [3] — addressing resize observer issues and missing overflow: auto / max-height constraints on the panel. To verify this is the issue: Try scrolling down i

title: Erro cokies · apache/superset · Discussion #39363 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Estou a tentar usar um dashboard no iframe tenho https em abos os lados. Se fizer sessao noutro separador e aceder o iframe funciona contudo senao fizer sessao ele dá erro. O iframe funciona em qualquer dispositivo desde que num dispositivo qualquer faça login , os restantes dispositvios n necessito fazer login. Faz uma checklist do que necessito verificar se está correto
author: Tranqu1lo
comment_excerpt: @Tranqu1lo , este é exactamente o mesmo problema que já discutimos nas threads anteriores [1] [2] — o dashboard funciona com login noutro separador porque o browser já tem cookies de sessão, mas sem login os cookies não chegam ao iframe. Aqui está a checklist completa para o superset_config.py : 1. Feature Flag FEATURE_FLAGS = {"EMBEDDED_SUPERSET": True} 2. Cookies de Sessão SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True ← usar True explícito, não USE_HTTPS — se a variável não resolver correctamente, os cookies são bloqueados pelo browser [2] SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = True 3. CORS ENABLE_CORS = True CORS_OPTIONS com o domínio exacto do seu site e supports_credentials: True : CORS_OPTIONS = { "origins" : [ "https://seu-site.com" ], "supports_credentials" : True , } 4. Content || Request URL https://superset/api/v1/chart/data?form_data=%7B7D&dashboard_id=99 Request method POST Status code 401 UNAUTHORIZED Existe CRSF token guest token. O iframe é mostrado o titulo contudo o chart não é autorizado. O servidor do superset indica csrf token esse erro é o que devovle 401. QUal é o erro principal ? || @Tranqu1lo , o facto de o iframe mostrar o título do dashboard mas o chart devolver 401 UNAUTHORIZED no /api/v1/chart/data indica que o guest token autentica o carregamento inicial do dashboard, mas as chamadas API subsequentes para dados dos charts falham. Isto geralmente tem duas causas possíveis: 1. Permissões do Guest Role — Esta é provavelmente a causa principal neste caso. O role configurado em GUEST_ROLE_NAME (por defeito "Public" ) precisa de ter estas permissões mínimas [1] : can_read em Dashboard , Chart , Dataset , Database can_read em CurrentUserRestApi ← muito comum esquecer este, e sem ele os dashboards embutidos falham [2] can_explore em Superset can_explore_json em Superset can_time_range em Api Recomendo criar um role dedicado (ex: "EmbedGuest" ) em vez de usar o Public :  || N existe para mim esta permissao can_readem CurrentUserRestApi || @Tranqu1lo , este é um problema reportado por outros utilizadores — em algumas versões do Superset (particularmente v6.1.0RC1+), a permissão can read on CurrentUserRestApi pode não aparecer na lista de permissões na UI [1] . Há duas formas de resolver: 1. Correr superset init — Este comando sincroniza as permissões e roles. Ele executa sync_role_definitions() que cria e actualiza as permissões disponíveis [2] : superset init Após correr, reinicie o Superset e verifique novamente se a permissão aparece na lista do role. 2. Procurar pelo ID da permissão — Alguns utilizadores reportam que a permissão existe mas não é facilmente encontrável na UI. Tente pesquisar por "CurrentUser" ou "me" no campo de pesquisa das permissões. A permissão pode aparecer como can read on CurrentUserRestApi [1] . P

title: Last date label hidden on Time Series X-axis · apache/superset · Discussion #39361 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: In Charts, when the X-axis is set to a Time format, the last date label often fails to display. This is particularly important for daily or hourly monitoring dashboards where the user needs to confirm the exact "latest" time reflected on the chart at a glance.
author: aijung0331
comment_excerpt: Hi @aijung0331 ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. This is a reported issue that has been addressed through several recent fixes. The root cause is ECharts' hideOverlap: true setting aggressively suppressing the last label when it detects potential overlap at the axis boundary [1] [2] . Several PRs have landed to fix this: PR #37755 — conditionally disables hideOverlap for time-based axes with rotated labels and adds dynamic right padding proportional to the rotation angle [1] PR #38017 — fixes the smart date formatter so forced max labels don't render as raw timestamps [3] PR #38733 — deduplicates X-axis labels that could appear twice at axis boundaries [4] If you're on an older version and can't upgrade yet, some workarounds: Rotate the X-axis labels (e.g., 45°) — this gives mo

title: Best Practices for Storing Superset Dashboards in Git · apache/superset · Discussion #30190 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi all, I'm exploring the best practices for storing Superset dashboards in Git for version control purposes. I haven’t come across many articles or discussions on this topic, so I wanted to reach out to the community for guidance. Currently, my team and I follow this approach: Export newly developed dashboards as a zip archive. We have a 'Superset' folder in our Git repo with subfolders for charts, dashboards, databases, and datasets. We then place the contents of the zip (YAML files) into the corresponding directories and remove the ID numbers from the filenames. (for example dashboard Legal_Information_40.yaml becomes Legal_Information.yaml ) Finally, we commit and push the changes. As a result, each folder has a bunch of YAML-files. We don't store zip-archives. Does this workflow align with best practices, or is there a more efficient way to handle version control for Superset dashboards? Any feedback, suggestions, or resources would be greatly appreciated! Thanks in advance!
author: ampil
comment_excerpt: Hey @ampil ! 👋 I'm here to help you with any questions or issues you have. I can assist with bugs, answer your questions, and even guide you on becoming a contributor. Let's tackle this together! The best practices for storing Superset dashboards in Git for version control include: Exporting Dashboards as YAML Files : Use the ExportDashboardsCommand to export dashboards to YAML files, which are more suitable for version control compared to binary formats like zip archives. Organizing Files into Subfolders : Organize the exported YAML files into logical subfolders (e.g., dashboards , charts , datasets , databases ) for better manageability. Removing ID Numbers from Filenames : Customize the filenames to exclude ID numbers if desired, using a consistent naming convention. How do you handle that? Do you just store a duplicate copy of the dataset in the repo under each dashboard? || Yes, that's possible. title: Vambery AI Agent — AI-powered SQL assistant extension for SQL Lab (public beta) · apache/superset · Discussion #38356 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: What is it? Vambery AI Agent is an AI-powered data assistant that lives inside SQL Lab as a sidebar panel. It uses Superset's native Extension System (Module Federation) — no core modifications needed. Ask questions in natural language, and the AI inspects your schema, writes SQL, executes queries, and creates charts — all without leaving SQL Lab. Named after Ármin Vámbéry — the Hungarian orientalist and polyglot who explored unknown territories. Like its namesake, Vambery navigates your data landscape so you don't have to. Features Natural language → SQL — describe what you need, the AI writes and executes the query Schema-aware — automatically inspects databases, schemas, tables, columns, comments, descriptions, and predefined metrics Interactive chart creation — creates bar, line, pie, and table charts from query results (preview or save) Auto-fill editor — sets the final query in SQL Lab and auto-executes Markdown responses — syntax-highlighted code blocks, tables, formatted text Streaming — real-time SSE with visible tool-use steps Multiple LLM providers — Azure OpenAI, OpenAI, OpenRouter Database support — MSSQL, PostgreSQL, and any SQL database Superset connects to How it works The extension registers a right sidebar panel in SQL Lab. The AI agent has access to 8 tools: Tool What it does list_schemas / list_tables
author: matebenyovszky
comment_excerpt: Really interesting to see text-to-SQL integrated directly into Superset's SQL Lab via the Extension System. The schema-awareness piece is what separates useful NL-to-SQL from toy demos — knowing which tables, columns, and relationships exist before generating a query eliminates the most common failure mode (hallucinated column names). A few questions on the architecture: Query validation layer — does Vambery validate the generated SQL against the schema before execution, or does it rely on the database to catch errors? A pre-execution AST check could save round-trips and give better error messages to users. Multi-dialect handling — Superset supports many database backends. How does the agent handle dialect-specific syntax (e.g., LIMIT vs TOP , date functions across Postgres/MySQL/BigQuery) || Thank you for the feedback and for your questions! Your tool is great! I wrote my first text2SQL tool last spring which supported multiple dialects, and last autumn it was able to create complex query plans, evaluate and update plan step by step and create a final report based on that. The main context and sample queries were fed into the context, and the main logic and steps were predefined (e.g. I gave samples to the AI how to create a plan etc.). In this extension I wanted to test the built-on "thinking" or reasoning approach of modern "tool using" LLM-s like GPT5, and to be honest it works really well! So far it solved all questions, however I still plan to enrich context with addition descriptions from the scheme or even add skills (so the AI would know how to use properly complex dat || Updated the codebase: Added functionality to detect SQL dialect based on the Superset Database model. Implemented SQL syntax validation using sqlglot to catch errors before execution. Updated system prompt to include database context and dialect-specific rules. GitHub: https://github.com/integrityauthority/superset-extensions Feature requests: https://github.com/integrityauthority/superset-extensions/issues/new?labels=enhancement&template=feature_request.md || I've just added Ollama support Keep sending request in https://github.com/integrityauthority/superset-extensions/issues || Now you can select from multiple providers: Edit former prompts: Try multiple suggestions from the AI: || Just added a new release which works with Superset 6.1.0 rc and the supx extension format https://github.com/integrityauthority/superset-extensions Ideas and feedback welcomed! || Vambery AI Agent v0.3 — expanded capabilities for Superset 6.1.x Major update to the Vambery AI Agent extension: Now with 15 agent tools (up from 8): Views support — the agent now discovers and uses database views alongside tables automatically Dataset management — list, inspect, and edit existing Superset datasets (descriptions, column metadata, SQL for virtual datasets) Chart management — list, inspect, and edit existing Superset charts Internal task planning — the agent breaks complex requests into steps and verifies each result, so it reliably completes tasks including chart creation Technical highlights: Targets Superset 6.1.x+ with .supx as the primary deployment format Uses Superset's internal Python APIs directly (Database, SqlaTable, Slice models) — no external MCP dependency need

title: Erro dashboard · apache/superset · Discussion #39343 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Tenho este erro This session has encountered an interruption, and some controls may not work as intended. If you are the developer of this app, please check that the guest token is being generated correctly. Se tiver login noutro separador mostra o grafico se tiver sem login ele dá o aviso de cima. É seguro fazer WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = False ? Ambos os sites têm https
author: Tranqu1lo
comment_excerpt: Este erro é exactamente o mesmo problema que foi discutido nesta thread recente — o dashboard embutido não consegue obter o CSRF token porque os cookies de sessão não estão a chegar ao Superset no contexto do iframe. Não é recomendado usar WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = False em produção — isso desativa a proteção CSRF para todas as rotas, o que expõe o Superset a ataques CSRF [1] . Como ambos os sites já têm HTTPS, a solução correcta é garantir que tem todas estas configurações no superset_config.py : FEATURE_FLAGS = { "EMBEDDED_SUPERSET" : True } # Cookies de sessão para cross-origin (iframe) SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True # Obrigatório com SameSite=None SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = True # CORS — substituir pelo domínio real do seu site ENABLE_CORS = True CORS_OPTIONS = { || O dashboard funciona se tiver login noutro separador caso contrario dá erro || O comportamento que descreve — funcionar com login noutro separador mas falhar sem login — é o sintoma clássico de que o dashboard embutido está a usar a sessão do browser em vez do guest token [1] . O que acontece: quando está com login noutro separador, o browser já tem um cookie de sessão válido e envia-o ao iframe. Sem esse login, os cookies de sessão não existem ou não estão a ser transmitidos ao iframe, e o guest token sozinho não consegue completar a autenticação porque o Superset precisa de cookies de sessão como mecanismo de transporte mesmo com guest tokens [2] . A causa mais provável é que os cookies de sessão não estão correctamente configurados para cross-origin . Verifique estas configurações no superset_config.py : SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = Tru

title: Dasboard embedded · apache/superset · Discussion #39200 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Estou usando a versão mais recente do Superset e consigo gerar o guest_token via API usando um usuário autenticado, mas ao tentar acessar um dashboard embutido via /embedded/ recebo o erro CSRF token is missing. Detalhes: Endpoint /api/v1/security/guest_token/ funciona e retorna o token corretamente. WTF_CSRF_ENABLED = True. Estou usando HTTP, não HTTPS. Cookies de sessão estão sendo enviados pelo PHP backend, mas aparentemente não correspondem ao CSRF esperado
author: Tranqu1lo
comment_excerpt: Esse erro de CSRF é bastante comum ao configurar dashboards embutidos. O endpoint /api/v1/security/guest_token/ não está na lista padrão de isenção de CSRF do Superset [1] , então mesmo que o token seja gerado corretamente, as requisições subsequentes ao dashboard embutido podem falhar se os cookies de sessão e o CSRF token não estiverem sincronizados. Aqui estão as configurações que provavelmente faltam no seu superset_config.py : 1. Isentar o endpoint de CSRF (mais simples para desenvolvimento): WTF_CSRF_EXEMPT_LIST = [ "/api/v1/security/guest_token" ] [2] 2. Configurar cookies de sessão para cross-origin (obrigatório para embedding): SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = False # False para HTTP, True para HTTPS SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = False ⚠️ Nota importante : Nave || Preciso de ter certificado no meu site ou apenas no superset ? Tendo o certificado de segurança do superset existe algum erro que possa acontecer no superset ? || Ambos precisam de HTTPS (certificado SSL) em produção. O motivo é que para dashboards embutidos funcionarem cross-origin, você precisa de SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" , e navegadores modernos exigem que SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True (ou seja, HTTPS) quando SameSite=None está configurado [1] . Isso se aplica tanto ao Superset quanto ao site que faz o embedding. Resumindo: ✅ HTTP nos dois — funciona apenas para desenvolvimento local ✅ HTTPS nos dois — recomendado para produção ❌ Misturar HTTP e HTTPS — não funciona, navegadores bloqueiam os cookies Quanto a erros que podem acontecer mesmo com HTTPS configurado no Superset: CSRF token missing — Mesmo com HTTPS, se você não buscar o CSRF token via /api/v1/security/csrf_token/ e incluí-lo no header X-CSRFToken das requisições POST, vai conti || Estou a usar dashboards embebidos no Apache Superset com guest token e estou a ter um problema de CSRF. 🔧 Configuração Superset com Embedded dashboards ativado Uso de guest token API (/api/v1/security/guest_token) Token é gerado com sucesso Já tentei WTF_CSRF_EXEMPT_LIST ❌ Problema Durante o carregamento do dashboard recebo nos logs: flask_wtf.csrf.CSRFError: 400 Bad Request: The CSRF token is missing || O seu caso é interessante porque o guest token é gerado com sucesso, mas o erro de CSRF acontece durante o carregamento do dashboard . Isso significa que o problema não está na geração do token, mas sim nas chamadas API que o dashboard faz ao renderizar. O dashboard embutido faz várias chamadas API além do guest token — por exemplo, /api/v1/me/roles/ , /api/v1/embedded_dashboard/ , entre outras [1] . A WTF_CSRF_EXEMPT_LIST com apenas o endpoint do guest token não cobre essas chamadas. Há duas coisas a verificar: 1. Configuração de cookies de sessão — Esta é provavelmente a causa raiz. Para embedding cross-origin, os cookies precisam ser configurados assim no superset_config.py : SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True # Requer HTTPS SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = True Se e || Apr 14 11:03:50 viz-tur bash[2812861]: flask_wtf.csrf.CSRFError: 400 Bad Request: The CSRF token is missing. Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: 2026-04-14 11:04:29,722:INFO:flask_wtf.csrf:The CSRF token is missing. Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: 2026-04-14 11:04:29,722:WARNING:superset.views.error_handling:Refresh CSRF token error Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: Traceback (most recent call last): Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: File "/app/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/flask_wtf/csrf.py", line 261, in protect Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: validate_csrf(self._get_csrf_token()) Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: File "/app/venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/flask_wtf/csrf.py", line 100, in validate_csrf Apr 14 11:04:29 viz-tur bash[2812858]: raise Val || Obrigado pelos logs — eles são úteis. A mensagem chave é Refresh CSRF token error , que vem do error handler global para CSRFError no Superset [1] . Isso significa que uma requisição (provavelmente feita pelo frontend do dashboard embutido) está a ser interceptada pela validação CSRF do Flask-WTF antes de chegar ao handler da view. O que está a acontecer é um ciclo problemático: o dashboard embutido tenta buscar um CSRF token via /api/v1/security/csrf_token/ , mas essa própria requisição falha com CSRF error, porque os cookies de sessão não estão a ser entregues ao Superset pelo browser. Isso geralmente acontece por causa da configuração de cookies. Pode partilhar a sua configuração atual de SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE , SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE , e se está a usar HTTP ou HTTPS? Se está a usar HT || SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = False SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = USE_HTTPS estou a usar https no site e no superset || Atenção — USE_HTTPS não é uma variável nativa do Superset [1] . Se não a definiu explicitamente no seu superset_config.py , essa linha vai dar NameError , ou se estiver definida como False por defeito noutro lado, SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE ficará como False — e os cookies não serão enviados corretamente em contexto cross-origin com SameSite=None . Confirme o valor real substituindo por: SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True Isso é obrigatório quando usa HTTPS com SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE = "None" — sem Secure=True , os browsers bloqueiam os cookies no iframe e o CSRF token da sessão nunca chega ao Superset, o que explica exatamente o erro que está a ver. Além disso, confirme que tem as configurações de CORS e CSP a apontar para o domínio do seu site: ENABLE_CORS = True CORS_OPTIONS = { "origins" : [ "ht || USE_HTTPS = True devolve um checklist com tudo o que é necessario para funcionar , o sitema chega a devolver o guest_token.

author: iceangel86868686-bot
comment_excerpt: Hi @iceangel86868686-bot ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. The issue you're running into is a common one — having translations in your .po and .json files doesn't automatically mean they'll show up in the UI. There are a few things to check: 1. Ensure both backend and frontend translations are compiled Superset uses two separate translation pipelines [1] . Even if your .po file has the translation for "Metrics," you need to make sure both compilation steps have been run: Backend ( .po → .mo ): pybabel compile -d superset/translations Frontend ( .po → .json ): cd superset-frontend npm run build-translation (cite:docs:afdb9d10:549-568) If you only updated the .po file but didn't recompile the .json , the frontend React components won't pick up those translations. Most of the UI l || I have compiled for the Russian language 3 errors were found, I open the file, and I don't understand what he doesn't like. It also says that everything should work fine. Okay let's continue One more question, there is no superset-frontend directory. I installed it via pip || Since you installed Superset via pip, the good news is that pre-compiled .mo and .json translation files should already be included in the pip package. The release process compiles both backend ( .mo ) and frontend ( .json ) translations before publishing to PyPI [1] . So if you check your ~/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/superset/translations/ru/LC_MESSAGES/ , you should already have both messages.mo and messages.json — and from your original screenshots, it looks like you do. About the 3 compilation errors: Those warnings from pybabel compile are usually about minor formatting issues in the .po file (like mismatched placeholders %s / %d , or encoding issues). If it says compilation otherwise succeeds, they typically don't block translations from working. About the missing superset-fro || I started executing commands and errors started appearing

title: How to enable Alerts/Reports/Thumbnails with SSO-only authentication in Superset? · apache/superset · Discussion #33654 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: We are running Superset in an enterprise environment with SSO-only authentication (OAuth). Problem: Alerts & Reports (including thumbnails/screenshots) are not working natively. The headless browser (we are using Playwright) is redirected to the SSO login page, so the reports/emails contain blank images or the login screen instead of the actual dashboard/chart. We have tried using Playwright scripts to automate the SSO login and capture screenshots, but is not working. Has anyone successfully enabled Alerts/Reports/Thumbnails in a SSO-only environment using Playwright or any other method? Are there any best practices, plugins, or supported workarounds for this scenario? Example: Environment: Superset version: 4.1.1 SSO provider: Auth0
author: marcosmamorim
comment_excerpt: Hi @marcosmamorim ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. Enabling Alerts, Reports, and Thumbnails in Superset with SSO-only (OAuth) authentication is a common challenge, as headless browsers like Playwright or Selenium are redirected to the SSO login page, resulting in blank or login screenshots. Here’s what is known and what you can try: 1. Why this happens: Superset’s async tasks (alerts, reports, thumbnails) impersonate a user context using an executor pattern. When generating screenshots, the worker tries to access dashboards as a specific user, but with SSO-only setups, the headless browser cannot easily authenticate, so it gets redirected to the login page instead of rendering the dashboard/chart [ source ]. 2. No official plugin or built-in workaround exists for SSO-only envi || The solution mentioned in #14330 (comment) seems to work. The problem is that the methods authenticate_webdriver and authenticate_browser_context in MachineAuthProvider redirect to the login-url.

comment_excerpt: Hi @anwesha-smsrc ! I'm Dosu and I’m helping the superset team. Superset doesn't have a built-in feature to automatically show/hide columns based on the current time period at the rendering layer. However, the best approach for your scenario is Jinja templating in your SQL query , which can dynamically control what columns appear without manual intervention. Using Jinja to conditionally include columns: You can use Jinja logic in a virtual dataset (SQL Lab query) or calculated column to check the current date and decide whether to include the bimonthly column. For example: SELECT id, four_monthly_value, mat_value, {% set current_month = modules . datetime . datetime . now ().month %} {% if current_month in [ 2 , 4 , 6 , 8 , 10 , 12 ] %} bimonthly_value {% else %} NULL AS bimonthly_value {% || I wasn't able to read the Excel file contents directly, but based on your description I understand the challenge: Your data is in a pivot structure — meaning the period names (e.g., "Jan-Feb", "Mar-Apr", etc.) are column headers , not values in a row. Columns B and C are calculated metrics that should only show values for alternate bimonthly periods . There's no separate "period" column in the data source to filter on. This is a tricky scenario. Here's how you can approach it: Step 1: Unpivot the data Since the periods are column names (pivot format), create a virtual dataset in SQL Lab that unpivots them into rows. For example (syntax varies by database): -- PostgreSQL example using UNION ALL SELECT ' Jan-Feb ' AS period, jan_feb_col_b AS metric_b, jan_feb_col_c AS metric_c FROM your_tabl || Company Rx JAN-FEB 2026 Rank JAN-FEB 2026 A || Thanks, now I see the structure clearly. So your data looks like: Company Rx JAN-FEB 2026 Rank JAN-FEB 2026 (Rx MAR-APR 2026) (Rank MAR-APR 2026) ... The period is embedded in the column name itself — there's no separate period column. The columns Rx JAN-FEB 2026 and Rank JAN-FEB 2026 are the calculated fields, and you want them to only appear during that bimonthly period. Here's the approach I'd recommend: Use Jinja with the current date to conditionally select the right columns. Since the period info is baked into column names, you can map the current month to the correct bimonthly column: {% set m = datetime . now ().month %} {% set y = datetime . now ().year %} {% if m in [ 1 , 2 ] %} {% set period = ' JAN-FEB ' ~ y %} {% elif m in [ 3 , 4 ] %} {% set period = ' MAR-APR ' ~ y %} {% eli || As I have mentioned earlier, it is not datetime column also the metric name is written manually @dosu || I understand — to clarify what I meant: the Jinja solution I proposed does not require a datetime column in your data. It uses datetime.now() purely as a Python function at query render time to figure out which string-named column to pick. Your data stays exactly as-is — string column names like "Rx JAN-FEB 2026" . However, I see the deeper issue now: the metric/column names are manually written in your Superset chart configuration (in the Metrics section), not dynamically selected. So even if the SQL can be made dynamic, you're defining the metric by hand in the chart builder as something like: Custom SQL: "Rx JAN-FEB 2026" And you'd have to manually change that every period. body_or_excerpt: BRANDING ON DOWNLOADED IMAGES — "Powered by xyz" (my feedback) Superset's export generates plain screenshots. This is solvable. how to do this for all image download and csv download @dosubot
author: srijani-smsrc
comment_excerpt: Superset does not have a built-in "Powered by xyz" branding feature for exports, so you'll need code modifications. Here's how to approach it for each export type: For image/screenshot exports , the best approach is to post-process images in the screenshot pipeline. Superset already uses PIL/Pillow for image resizing in superset/utils/screenshots.py [1] , so you can add a watermark step after capture: from PIL import Image , ImageDraw , ImageFont from io import BytesIO def add_watermark ( image_bytes ): img = Image . open ( BytesIO ( image_bytes )) draw = ImageDraw . Draw ( img ) font = ImageFont . truetype ( "Arial.ttf" , 20 ) draw . text (( 10 , img . height - 30 ), "Powered by xyz" , fill = "gray" , font = font ) output = BytesIO () img . save ( output , format = "PNG" ) return output . || def add_watermark(image_bytes): img = Image.open(BytesIO(image_bytes)) draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img) font = ImageFont.truetype("Arial.ttf", 20) draw.text((10, img.height - 30), "Powered by xyz", fill="gray", font=font) output = BytesIO() img.save(output, format="PNG") return output.getvalue() exactly where to add this in the screenshots file in superset5 @dosubot || In superset/utils/screenshots.py , there are two places you should consider, depending on what you want to watermark: Option 1 (Recommended) — In compute_and_cache() , after the screenshot is captured but before caching (around line 270) This is the best spot because it watermarks the full-resolution image before any resizing for thumbnails. title: Query issue · apache/superset · Discussion #39309 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Divison operation is not working in oracle query after upgradation from superset 4.1,4 to v 6.0.0 what could be the issue? DB engine Error Could not convert string '0' to numeric This may be triggered by: Issue 1011 - Superset encountered an unexpected error. Please reach out to the Chart Owner for assistance. Chart Owner: Admin User
author: asishPatnaik2000
comment_excerpt: @dosu any update on this? || Hi @asishPatnaik2000 — I looked into this further. Your original issue ("Could not convert string '0' to numeric" during division operations on Oracle) is separate from the positional GROUP BY issue discussed earlier in this thread, so upgrading sqlglot alone wouldn't fix it. There's no exact match for this error in existing issues, but here are some likely causes and things to check: Check the generated SQL — In SQL Lab or the chart's "View Query" option, look at the actual query Superset generates. Oracle handles type coercion differently than other databases, and a division operation where one operand is implicitly treated as a string (e.g., from a VARCHAR2 column or a calculated field) would cause this error. Explicit CAST in your metric/column — If your division involves columns that  || @dosu cant share generated query for company policies I ca give an example: ROUND( MAX(CASE WHEN some_type = 'X' THEN TO_NUMBER(some_value) END) / NULLIF((SELECT TO_NUMBER(pref_value) FROM some_preferences WHERE pref_key = 'FACTOR'), 0), 2 ) ethe divison works when run directly through sql lab || Thanks for the example and the clarification — that's very helpful. The fact that the division works in SQL Lab but fails in a chart is a strong clue. The root cause is likely how Superset wraps chart queries differently from SQL Lab. SQL Lab executes your SQL directly against the database [1] , but when rendering a chart, Superset compiles your metric expression into a SQLAlchemy query object, then wraps it in subqueries for limits, filtering, and post-processing [2] . This wrapping can alter how Oracle interprets type conversions — your TO_NUMBER() calls and the division may lose their context when nested inside Superset's generated subquery layers, causing Oracle to treat a numeric result as a string. Since Oracle's engine spec doesn't define custom type mutators [3] , the error is almo || @dosu we already tried this did not work CAST( ROUND( MAX(CASE WHEN some_type = 'X' THEN TO_NUMBER(some_value) END) / NULLIF((SELECT TO_NUMBER(pref_value) FROM some_preferences WHERE pref_key = 'FACTOR'), 0), 2 ) AS NUMBER ) and we checked all the data are valid it is working/charts are coming through superset admin but when rendered through iframe user fetting this issue || This is a really important detail — it works as admin in Superset but fails when rendered via iframe . That changes the diagnosis significantly. This is likely not a pure Oracle division issue but rather a problem with how Superset generates queries in embedded/guest token mode . Here are the most probable causes: 1. Double RLS application bug (Superset 6.0.0 regression) If you're using Row Level Security rules in your guest token, there's a known regression in 6.0.0 where RLS filters get applied twice — once wrapping the query in a subquery and again as a WHERE clause on the outer query. This double-wrapping can break type context for your division expression, causing Oracle to misinterpret numeric values as strings. This was introduced by PR #36061 .
