author: d9k
comment_excerpt: As a temporary poor man's solution I've added custom functions for jinja2 templates to escape PrestoDb strings. These functions use replacement of single quote with CHR(39) concatenation: > escape_presto_string("Dr's") # "Dr' || CHR(39) || 's" def escape_presto_string(s = ''): return s.replace("'", "' || CHR(39) || '") # >> escape_presto_strings(["Dr's", 't']) # ["Dr' || CHR(39) || 's", 't'] def escape_presto_strings(ar = []): return list(map(escape_presto_string, ar)) # >> escape_presto_strings_in(["Dr's", 't']) # "'Dr' || CHR(39) || 's','t'" def escape_presto_strings_in(ar = []): escaped_ar = escape_presto_strings(ar) if (len(escaped_ar) == 0): return "" return "'" + "','".join(escaped_ar) + "'" JINJA_CONTEXT_ADDONS = { "escape_presto_string": escape_presto_string, "escape_presto_strings || So code above becomes: {% set filter_city = filter_values("example_filter_city_name", remove_filter = True) %} {% if filter_city | length %} example_city_name IN ({{ escape_presto_strings_in(filter_city) }}) OR example_city_name IS NULL {% else %} TRUE {% endif %} || I want to improve escape_presto_string() function by calling data source "native" escape function. How to get current sql alchemy connection in escape_presto_string() and which methods should I call to escape string value? || I think it's good practise to deny requests with non printable characters and check field length. For example, mysql-real-escape-string function escapes \x00, \n, \r, , ', " and \x1a characters but people usually don't have || @andrey-mikhailov don't have what? mysql-real-escape-string function Superset uses python, not php || I know it's an older topic, but since this didn't provide the answer and I've found it since, I figured that for future people looking for the same solution I figured I'd post what I found. You can escape Jinja parameters/variables using standard Jinja syntax (see: https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/3.1.x/templates/#html-escaping ). Simply add |e to the end of the variable for which you want to escape the characters. Sample code that allows you to search partial matches of a list of values provided in a dashboard filter, but escapes the unsafe characters like the single quote: 0 %} AND ({% for value in filter_values('Status', remove_filter=True) %} {% if value %} upper(t.status_desc) LIKE upper('{{ "%" + value|e + "%" }}') {% endif %}{% if not loop.last %} OR {% endif %} {% endfor %} ) { || Thanks, it looks to work well to me for some malcious inputs. I'm worrying about its completeness. According to the doc, |e filter looks HTML escaping. Is it enough for SQL? || It should be very important point to use Jinja templates. I consider that the Superset doc should mention that using Jinja Template is vulnerable to SQL injection so we need manual escaping or {% autoescape True %} . || I completely agree with you. Also, I am not a security or pen test expert, just speaking from the limited exposure to this I've had. I recall having read an article somewhere that talks about creating a custom function and adding it to Superset so that it can clean inputs. That's not an option for everyone. With how vulnerable Jinja templating is to SQL injection, there probably should be indeed an option to enable or disable it/apply it. In any case, HTML escaping just happens to escape the single quote, which is one of the main offenders in SQL injection. Jinja templating can still be applied safely if all you're using it for is generating SQL code blocks that do NOT use a Jinja templating variable inline. Another thing to consider is using low permission accounts for anything that conne || merge this into your supsert_config.py import re from jinja2 import Environment, FileSystemLoader # --- Regex Compilation (Optimization) --- # Compile patterns once when the module is loaded. _ALLOWED_PATTERN = re.compile(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9 / \\\\ { } \* & % $ # @ ! = : \' \(\) \[\] -]') _COMBINED_BLACKLIST_PATTERN = re.compile(r';|--|/\*|\*/') # --- Custom Jinja Filter Definitions --- def _custom_alphanum_symbol_only(value): """ Processes a string for direct inclusion as a string literal within PostgreSQL. Combines whitelisting, blacklist detection, and SQL single-quote escaping. Steps: 1. Whitelist characters: Removes any characters not in the allowed set. 2. Detect whitelist changes: If characters were removed, indicates unsafe input (returns None). 3. Detect blacklist patterns: Checks for k

title: Enhancement Suggestion: Add ‘Copy Tab’ and ‘Copy to Another Tab’ Functionality · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #16179 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi everyone 👋 I’ve been contributing to Lightdash over the past few days and exploring different parts of the application. While working on some issues related to tabs, I realized it might be really useful to have functionality for copying entire tabs , including the content within them (like charts, tables, etc.), and also just the specific content as well, if needed. Currently, we have options like Move to another tab for content within tab, but I think it would be helpful to also support actions like Copy to another tab or Duplicate tab for tabs. This could allow users to quickly transfer or reuse content from one tab to another without going through the repetitive process of manually adding charts and tables again. In some cases, users might even want to duplicate the entire tab layout to start from an existing context instead of building everything from scratch. PS: I checked the existing issues but couldn't find anything related to this, apologies if I missed something or if this has already been discussed before! Would love to hear your thoughts on this!

While working on it, I thought it might also be useful to have the ability to copy specific content from a tab and paste it into another tab (as mentioned in the discussion above as well), wherever the user wants it in the tab. I'm not entirely sure if this would be a valuable feature, but it feels somewhat relevant. Have you received any user requests for something like this? Or could you possibly discuss it with the team to see if it's something we might want to consider?

title: Stop all data collection by Lightdash company (https://www.lightdash.com/) · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #13992 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I have a self-hosted instance of lightdash with docker.io/lightdash/lightdash:latest . I have not created an account on the lightdash cloud service: https://www.lightdash.com/ However, when I created an account on lightdash (self-hosted instance), I received a welcome email, not from my server but a lightdash.com email. The instance remained dormant for a while but today I started populating data to it and got this creepy mail: So, you folks know exactly when I create a dbt project? Now I am concerned how much access does lightdash have and what all data is collected. Do you folks have access to my private databases? Do you folks have access to my IP address? Do you folks have access to my self-hosted instance's password? Do you folks have access to my configuration in docker.env file? (including but not limited to LIGHTDASH_SECRET , SMTP configurations etc?) Did I misconfigure something or is this the default level of data collection? How can I opt out-of any and all telemetry & data collection by any organization (directly or indirectly associated with lightdash)? Please don't share a link to your privacy policy, I am a software developer not a legal expert, I can be easily fooled with T&Cs buried in those pages. I am just looking to understand if the above data or any personally identifiable data is collected from self-hosted instances as well. Nothing personal. It's just creepy af.
author: atb00ker
comment_excerpt: Hey @atb00ker - when you sign up for a Lightdash account (self-hosted or Cloud), you're prompted on whether you want to receive Lightdash update emails - this is an opt-in option, if you opt-out, then you don't receive any emails. We don't have any access to any sensitive information from your account, we receive anonymised telemetry if it's not deactivated in the self-hosted instance - you can set the variable RUDDERSTACK_ANALYTICS_DISABLED to True in your environment variables to switch this off. https://docs.lightdash.com/self-host/customize-deployment/environment-variables || Thanks a lot!

title: Lightdash as an open access data visualisation platform? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #9836 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi everyone, I'm on a mission to find the best platform for a little open source project that I'm working on. I'm looking to piece together a data visualisation platform for sharing (in the public domain) datasets related to sustainable finance. The idea is that anyone who accesses the website would be able to access a frontend connected to the datasets and play around with the data (and download CSV pulls of what they come up with). The frontend users of course would need to have only read-only permission to the master databases. On the backend, I'd manage the connections to those (etc). I'm not sure (from a userflow perspective) which of these two pathways makes more sense: -> Total "open access" - anyone can access the play with the data with the knowledge that whatever they create is ephemeral and will be overwritten as soon as somebody else "joins in" (or simultaneously!) -> A middle ground. Anyone can register for a self-service account. The account is also read only versus the backing database but the users can save their own visualisations because ... it's their account. Ideally the account would be prepopulated with a set visualisation (such as a dashboard) that is "fixed" and can't be modified. The intention of this would be really as a teaser: here's what you can create. Get going! My question: can either of these setups be implemented with reasonable ease? Many thanks!

Currently we are using Google Data Studio as a embbed solution. It's very limited in the embbed functionality. But the main reason that we are moving away of it is because it experiencing contant downtimes. We decided to go with something open source that we can contribute with and also to host in our own servers. Recently we tried Metabase. The embbed functionality allows for passing query params that are applied directly to the SQL queries, witch is great. Also, it allows for generation of authenticated links for embbed through the back-end, while Google Data || Hi @TuringLovesDeathMetal , I second @bruno-berchielli as we have the exact same use case. Happy to test it out too! || Is there any update on this feature? I dont see it in the public roadmap. Thanks. || Hi @TuringLovesDeathMetal & @owlas ! I asked one of our UX folks at my current employer for thoughts on what good embedding would look like and one of the things they said which would be important in making an embedded product work well is ... customizable fonts (and UI elements like drop downs). I hadn't considered fonts to be related to embedding, but... maybe, it is. If the embedded product could inherit styling (fonts, colors, etc) from the host website either through manual configuration or (ideally) something automated, would be a huge leg up for Lightdash in an embedded use case over its competitors. I'm not sure if this is easy to do in iframes, and I'm not sure if there are non-iframe alternatives which would make this easy (a very cursory glance at echarts docs suggest css inheri

title: Can I move a dashboard between projects? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #3408 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: I generally test and build my dashboards on development branch (and project), then once the code is merged to production, find that I need to re-build the dashboard. Is there a way to move it to the production project when done?
author: risafangs
comment_excerpt: There is a way - but it’s not pretty. We can use the Lightdash API directly, we can basically scrape all the charts from the dev project and recreate them in the prod project We have an open issue for being able to move things between projects more easily here - would love to hear your thoughts on it! 😊 : #3014 What's the reason for building dashboards + charts in development first? Is it to test them out? Or is it because you're waiting for the data to get into production? Or is it some other reason? || You basically nailed the reason - the data is pretty new/fast-changing/spotty and I am testing first. Generally speaking, since production is tied to the main/master branch, I would probably be testing anything new on dev branches anyway, so I imagine this would continue to be an issue with in-development models even after the data is more established. || cool - makes sense. Testing first seems to be the most common use case for people (which is good to know when we consider how we'll build this 👀 ). for now though, it's only available through this hacky API approach. If you emoji-vote on the issue I posted above, I'm pretty sure you'll get updated on its progress!

title: Can I set the order that string values are shown in a visualization? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #2560 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: I have a simple Salesforce bar chart with counts of opportunities on the Y axis and the Salesforce stage on the X axis - things like Qualifying , Needs Analysis , Negotiating , Closed Won , etc. Would love to show them in pipeline stage order instead of descending by count. Is this possible to specify, or should do I need to do something like add a case statement to the SQL that numbers the stages? (eg case when Qualifying then '0 Qualifying' when Needs Analysis then '1 Needs Analysis' etc.
author: risafangs
comment_excerpt: Hi @risafangs - yes, you'd need to add something like a case statement to the SQL that numbers the stages for now. This is a really interesting question though! Typically, a funnel chart would only ever decrease as you went through steps, but I'm assuming your chart doesn't? 🤔 Out of interest, is there any other app you've used where you've been able to override the ordering in a funnel so it doesn't just order by decreasing size? Would love to see how other folks have done it! || The Salesforce data usually only includes the "current" stage of the opportunity (vs multiple entries as one record goes through all the stages), so I can't rely on it to self-order. I'm thinking about graphing in pandas/seaborn where you can specify the order of values in your visualizations. || Ah, makes sense. hmmm, that's a bit annoying that they don't give you multiple entries per record. But yes, either adding a case statement to append numbers to your steps and using Lightdash, or using pandas/seaborn where you can manually define the ordering might be the best options!

title: Make it easier to get started with Lightdash · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1156 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: 💡 What’s the idea? Currently this is more of a problem than a solution. It's very difficult to get started with Lightdash, especially if you have a bare-bones dbt project. Some areas of pain: The app forces you to connect to dbt successfully and compile your project before you can get any value out of Lightdash. Issues we've had is if people want to try Lightdash but have forgotten to fix something in dbt. Maybe they haven't upgraded to dbt 1.0.0 yet. The app gives very little content if you don't have dbt models. Many users have connected Lightdash to dbt but are only just getting started, they don't see any explores and the sql runner shows no tables in their warehouse. It's not possible to create a simple chart. Lightdash forces users to document dimensions in schema.yml files, even though a large proportion of new users don't bother documenting all the dimensions they'd like to access in Lightdash. There are a few paths to improvement here: Loosen restrictions on dbt project to get value for Lightdash. Allow user's to signup and create org before successfully compiling dbt. Allow user's to build charts from raw queries. We already have pre-defined metrics, these could be smarter, offering suggested metrics and queries for simple cases like a table that's been detected as containing users Some suggestions on the home page about ways to get started, including deep links into the starting place in the app. The onboarding screen currently ejects users from the app We could help users to generate dbt schemas / Lightdash meta using the app, so if you've only just got started with dbt, we can help document your models quicker 🧭 How will this move the needle on our key metric(s)? I think this well greatly reduce frustration by giving users value (seeing available data to query and creating charts) without restricting them to successfully connecting a warehouse AND dbt AND have dbt compile AND have no Lightdash meta errors. 🧮 How will we measure if this is successful? Improved onboarding/activation 🧘 Do we need to build confidence in this idea before we build it? 📝 What’s the rough overview of work required to ship this? ⏭ What are the next steps? ❓ What questions do we have for our users/community? 😎 Any other notes/comments/important info
author: owlas

You can now set a default chart colour palette by specifying hex colours in the Appearance tab under Settings . Always stay on-brand! Company updates Metrics and the Modern Data Stack 📊 Catch CTO Oliver at the Metrics Store Summit where he will be on a panel of experts discussing how the metrics layer will impact analyst workflows, both in the short term and the future! title: How do you handle feature requests from users? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1236 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: This is something that we have to think about in any product team: How do you handle feature requests from users? I've talked with a few different people about how this works in their teams, and taken a stab at writing down what this (generally) looks like at Lightdash. Would love to hear it though: 🤔 How do you handle feature requests from users at your company? ⚡ Here's how we do it at Lightdash: The summary is: we're pretty flexible and there are no 100% certain rules to follow here. The general guidelines we have are: When we get a feature request, we talk to the user to really understand their request and spend time giving a thoughtful response. Do we understand the problem they have? Are there any outstanding questions we’d have for building this? Is there a way we could guide them a bit so they could build it themselves? We add user feedback to Productboard - this way we know how popular a request is If their request is breaking Lightdash for them, we prioritize it quickly (this is for bugs more than feature requests) If tons of people are asking for it, or if people are saying “it’s the reason why they’re not switching”, we seriously consider building it sooner rather than later. We ask ourselves: Is it more important than the work we’ve lined up in the next few weeks? If it’s already related to what we’re working on, we probably will chuck it in with the other features we’re building (especially if it’s a small change). Other than these few guidelines, we’ll play it by ear 😊
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: The experience of syncing Lightdash with dbt should change. · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1143 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: ❓ What questions do we have for our users/community? When do you use the refresh dbt button? When was the last time you got an error from clicking refresh dbt ? What was it? When you've refreshed dbt, do you ever have to leave the Lightdash app to find information about the refresh? (debugging an error, seeing if it's actually syncing, etc.) Which step in making changes in dbt --> seeing them in Lightdash takes you the longest to complete right now? 💡 What’s the idea? Step 1: we automatically refresh dbt when there are changes in your GitHub connection The problem: the refresh dbt button is confusing. When do I press it? Why should I press it vs. running a query? We can remove this button to make it less confusing to users and make the connection to dbt way cleaner if we automatically refresh dbt for you when we detect changes in your GitHub project. We could make a CI/CD job - a GitHub action. When there's a merge to the branch your Lightdash project is connected to, it will compile your project and send this manifest.json to Lightdash. This removes the refresh dbt button. Step 2: we revamp the dbt connection page The problem: it's hard to debug dbt connection problems right now. Most of the errors that users run into come from their connection with their dbt project (both in onboarding + continuously). It should be easier to debug your Lightdash connection to dbt and understand the current state of your dbt project in Lightdash (when was it last synced? What commit has it synced to? What are the errors?) We create a page with information about the status of the dbt connection. we tell you the commit that your Lightdash project is synced to We tell you when the most recent Lightdash sync was done (i.e. when your project was last re-compiled) You get the logs of the refresh coming in as it's happening and once it's completed. You could see a history of compiles and other details about your connection (à la dbt cloud run history. or dataform’s run history) We give you the errors + warnings from the most recent compile. We also give you actions to solve the most common ones: do you have a dimension that was added to your dbt project, but we can't find it in the warehouse? Step 3: we check if the changes your making will break Lightdash. If you open a PR, then we check to see if this will work with your Lightdash project: we'd have a GitHub action that runs dbt compile it sends the manifest to your Lightdash instance - wouldn’t change what’s in Lightdash, but would compile manifest and send you back the state and tell you if you were going to break stuff in Lightdash. (we'd need to build an API with an endpoint that could test the changes in Lightdash) 📝 What’s the rough overview of work required to ship this? do we need user research? do we need to plan/partner with another company? do we need to find a new tool to do the thing we want to do? user research with a prototype/early sketch of what we want in there (show some example errors + some examp

title: Interactivity with charts + dashboards · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1138 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: ❓ What questions do we have for our users/community? When was the last time you used a dashboard filter? What did you use it for? What's your favourite chart/dashboard interactivity feature of your current (or past) BI tool? 💡 What’s the idea? Things like click-to-filter, drill-down and dashboard filters. Some people have said they wouldn’t switch BI tools until this exist. Dashboard level filters have come up a lot in feedback. Interactivity has come up with bigger users who are looking to switch. E.g. I have a chart with top 10 products by number of sales and under that I have total spend per day. If I click on boots, it would filter all of the other charts to boots. Drill-down hasn’t been requested as much as the dashboard filters. This is for any chart (both saved charts + saved charts in dashboards). If you click on an item in a chart, it shows you all of the values in that chart item (e.g. total number of users by status - click ‘active’, would show you all of the user IDs) Dashboard-level filters is the highest priority. Drill down into charts is the next highest priority. Click to filter also feels like it fits in with the other two. 📝 What’s the rough overview of work required to ship this? do we need user research? do we need to plan/partner with another company? do we need to find a new tool to do the thing we want to do? Research into other BI tool interactivity and what the possibilities are. Get examples (especially visual ones) of what this could look like in the app + how people would interact with this (what flows are there?) Talk to the people who were looking for this feature (dashboard filters) Deciding what’s the MVP version of this big feature? (this should just be a written proposal with words + some pictures) Design Engineering ⏭ What are the next steps? Research! 😎 Any other notes/comments/important info It feels like these three features are all related - they feel like different ways of interacting with dashboard-level filters (or chart level filters).

title: Tutorial videos for Lightdash · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1154 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: 💡 What’s the idea? We've had great feedback from user's about our video tutorials for example the dbt metrics tutorial on loom . We've had less positive feedback about our technical documentation. I think our tutorials and getting started guides in our documentation would be better as videos not as text. We can still keep our reference documentation and more technical documentation. Some great inspiration is Webflow's university 🧘 Do we need to build confidence in this idea before we build it? I don't think so, we've experimented with tutorial videos with great results. We should track how many questions we get 📝 What’s the rough overview of work required to ship this? To start, we should take our existing tutorials and convert those into Setting up Lightdash Exploring data Creating dimensions Creating metrics Joining tables I think there are some missing videos that we can create. A combination of product tutorials as well as best practice: What are metrics? How to be a great BI Engineer / analyst? How to help your business win with Lightdash. Advanced exploring (creating table calculations) Customising charts Using SQL Runner These tutorials can stay as text Deploy to production Updating docker image Eventually it would be great to organise the videos into courses and gamify the content a little. For now the existing docs.lightdash.com will work with embedded videos uploaded to YouTube/loom ⏭ What are the next steps? Shoot the first video! ❓ What questions do we have for our users/community? Are there tutorials that don't work as video? How do you currently learn about new lightdash features? Any new tutorials you'd love to see? 😎 Any other notes/comments/important info

title: Why we're building Lightdash on dbt · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1096 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: This post was originally written by @hamzahc1 ! (I just copied it over from Discourse 😉 ) Great data visualisation needs a great data transformation layer. So we built Lightdash on top of dbt. Introducing such a huge technical dependency wasn't an easy choice but we're really excited to build on dbt and here's why: SQL is the language of data We're building on open-source We're plugging into a standard It's better to do one thing and do it well Communities win together SQL is the language of data 🔤 Under the hood, dbt is simply running SQL statements. That's amazing because SQL has been around for 47 years and isn't going anywhere. SQL has a thriving community with a lot of collective knowledge on how to solve most common problems. Unlike scripting languages such as python, SQL is also easy to understand making it easy for engineers and Excel whizzes to collaborate 🤝. With Lightdash we threw away any ideas for a domain-specific language, and stuck with SQL. With Lightdash and dbt, analysts can write in SQL and their users then simply pick from the pre-defined metrics. We're building on open-source 🌍 If you're relying on any technical component to be a crucial part of your tech stack, it's a huge benefit if that component is open source. dbt's core is completely open-source 1 . By having access to the source code, you are protected in case the code owners decide to stop support. More exciting, you get the chance to contribute to the road map or extend the code however you see fit. Lightdash is open-source at it's core meaning you can see how we write queries, handle credentials, and enforce security. It enables us to be completely transparent and also lowers the barrier for our users to request features and report bugs. We're plugging into a standard 🔌 dbt has changed the way that we think about data pipelines. Before dedicated transformations tools, everyone was building their own pipeline DAGs and testing infrastructure with no standardisation. It was a huge engineering headache for those that remember the dark days. As of a year ago, over 2,000 companies using it weekly with 3x growth YoY 2 . If somebody today is building an analytics stack, they're likely using dbt somewhere. By plugging into this standard, Lightdash enables people to use their existing familiar tools and invest in the future. And if you throw Lightdash away, you are still left an industry-standard set of data transformations. It's better to do one thing and do it well 🥇 dbt follows the unix philosophy of doing one thing and doing it well (it also has a cute name à la awk , sed ). By just focusing on the "T" in ELT, dbt has become a powerful tool for a huge variety of use cases. Simply: it helps manage batch SQL-based transformations, in an idempotent way, and it just works. By leveraging dbt, Lightdash has a first-class transformation layer. With more and more people using dbt, and the fact that dbt does this bit so well, we thought it was time for a BI tool to be adding to t
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: Why are we building a community? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1095 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Before I started thinking about how to build the Lightdash community, I did the first thing everyone does when starting a project: I Googled it. Luckily, one of the first results to come up from Googling "How to build a community" was this blog post by Claire Carroll - a great read. The key takeaway for me was: "before you decide to spend time building a community, figure out why you want to build a community." So, I wanted us to get some thoughts down around our mission and beliefs as a team and figure out whether building a community would make sense for us (and you can probably guess what we decided 😏). Here's some of the things we came up with: We want to empower our users to get the best possible answers to their data questions. We believe that: teams are more likely to find better solutions than individuals. context is really important when you're trying to solve a problem. unless there's a good reason not to, information should be openly shared. business users shouldn't have to rely on a data person to be able to answer their questions. a BI tool should be easy to use and look really cool, so people are excited to use it. Aside from getting really excited about a super rad looking product 👩‍🎤, we talked quite a bit about "teams" of people and "teamwork" in our mission and beliefs. Empowering users with context, teams, and tools so that they can solve their data problems themselves is really important to us. After writing this up, we all agreed that having a community was going be a big part of how we built this product. Having a Lightdash community aligned really well with what we'd written down and we think that it'll empower our users to get the best possible answers to their data questions. So here we are, on our way to building a Lightdash community. We hope you'll join us for the ride. 🎢
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: The Lightdash Way: Data Access · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1093 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Our mission at Lightdash is: To enable everybody in your company to answer their own questions using data. Lightdash is where the data team (👷‍♀️ the builders) and the rest of the business (👩‍🌾 the consumers) come together to make better data-driven decisions. We have some pretty strong opinions about how this relationship between data builders and data consumers should work. So, we thought it would be useful to explain the principles that make up The Lightdash Way. The Lightdash Way isn't just semantics (😏) - it's about the entire experience of enabling everybody in your company to answer their own data questions. But, we'll start off slow and just go through one of the principles for now: How should you serve data to people who want to answer their own data questions? ⚡️ The Lightdash Way: Give end users meaningful building blocks to answer their own data questions. Let me explain what we mean... We've come across three ways to let people answer their questions using data: I hope it's obvious which one is our fave 😇 1. You give everyone access to the raw data. 🤪 If everybody in your organization knows SQL and everyone has the context they need to query the raw data (...and unicorns were real 🦄...), then giving everyone access to the raw data wouldn't be that bad. But, if a single person doesn't know SQL then they're totally dependent on someone helping them with a data question...no bueno. 👍: very flexible, totally self-serve (as long as everyone knows SQL) 👎: everybody has to know sql, everybody needs context of raw data, duplicated work, bound to be errors 2. If you have a data question, you ask the data builders, and they just give you complete answers. 🎁 If nobody knows SQL or they aren't willing to explore data themselves, then dedicated team members (probably data analysts) will be completely responsible for exploring data. Everybody relies on the data team for answer questions. 👍: only analysts have to have data skills, fewer errors (since only data experts are answering data questions) 👎: doesn't scale, analysts become a bottleneck, this kind of work sort of sucks :( 3. The data builders give you meaningful building blocks to answer your own data questions. 🧱 ⚡ The Lightdash Way: We give your data team the tools they need to build metrics + dimensions that everyone else can use in a user-friendly interface. With this approach, you leave the SQL to the experts: your data team. They become force-multipliers when they create these pre-defined metrics + dimensions in SQL because anybody in the business can combine, segment, and filter them to answer their own questions. The downside is that the data team need to spend some time defining and maintaining the library of metrics. But, a small set of metrics can power a huge amount of different analyses, enabling the rest of your team to answer their own data questions. 👍: you only need a few SQL experts, anyone can ask questions using the data, fewer errors, scaleable 👎: you need a data team, t

title: 2021/11/19 Roundup - Did you hear the one about the statistician? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1090 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Did you hear the one about the statistician? Probably... 🥁 🚄 Latest product updates :woman_mage: Pre-built metrics to get you started We've made it even easier to get started with Lightdash. We now provide example metrics for tables where you haven't got any defined yet. For now we've stuck to simple examples to help you get started, but watch this space, we've got big plans for this feature! 🌻 ✅ Only show the tables you want to work with You can now pick which dbt models you want to show in Lightdash. So you can ensure your team is only using the relevant tables and models and nothing else! You can choose to filter by tags or individually select the models you want. 🏃‍♀️ Run SQL queries in Lightdash You can now run raw SQL queries using our SQL Runner and visualise the results as a table in Lightdash! This one is for the SQL connoisseurs, you can now write and run your own queries right in Lightdash. And yes, we included autocomplete so you don't have to remember every table and column name 😅. Other notable updates: Charts can now be exported as an eCharts JSON Added support for Databricks Loads of bugs crushed and UX improvements As always, you can get the full story on changes we’ve made to Lightdash in our Changelog . 📰 What we're writing and reading Amy Chen from dbt Labs and Katie went to the OSA Conference and talked about the ways that open source drives innovation using a case study with dbt + Lightdash . The video from that talk is now available online . We published a short piece on " The Lightdash Way " that explains some of our core beliefs when it comes to building Lightdash. Monzo's Head of Data Science, Mikkel, wrote a great article on "moldy data" and how it can affect data teams, we enjoyed this a lot! We’re hiring - build the future of BI We have a bunch of open engineering positions right now to join us on our mission to help everyone answer their data questions (a small feat, we know). We're also offering a hefty referral bonus if you know anyone who would be a good fit! We’re looking for people who: think about their users are comfortable working autonomously but love being a part of a team are up for getting involved wherever they’re needed most. 100% remote and we’re open to all levels and abilities 👀 Here’s our job board If this sounds interesting to you or you know someone who would be a good fit, give us a shout! title: 2021/09/27 Roundup - There are 10 kinds of people in this world... · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1087 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: There are 10 kinds of people in this world... those who understand binary and those who don't. Latest Product Updates Below are some of the latest updates we've been making to Lightdash: 🔭 Lightdash works even when not all your models are ready We now compile all your explores individually so if there is an error with one, you can still use Lightdash for all the others. The UI will highlight any explores where there were errors with compiling so you can easily spot them. 🧪 We've added support for dbt projects in GitLab Got your dbt projects on GitLab? Now we've got you covered, simple! 🔧 You can edit your project settings in Lightdash The first of a few updates coming to make it easier to manage Lightdash - you can now see your dbt connection details in Lightdash and update your connection settings directly in the app. 🧮 Table calculations demo Check out Oliver's demo of one of our most requested features, table calculations where he walks through some examples of why this is so powerful for our users! News & Other Updates Catch us at Coalesce! Our very own Katie will be presenting at Coalesce this year, where she'll be talking about building your data pipelines with the open-source modern data stack. With her experience building pipelines at Monzo and now with Lightdash, this is one not to miss! Register here. We’re hiring - build the future of BI We have a bunch of open positions right now to join us on our mission to build the future of business intelligence on top of the modern data stack. We value being a small, high-performing team and operate 100% remote. If that gets you excited, we’d love to hear from you. Open roles: Full-stack Developer Frontend Engineer Product UI/UX designer You made it to the end! You are truly our biggest fan and we appreciate you. Katie and the Lightdash team ❤️
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: 2021/10/09 Roundup - What did the data say to the CPU? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1086 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: What did the data say to the CPU? cache me outside ...(how bout dat) Latest product updates Here are just 3 of the most popular features we’ve released in the last 2 weeks: 🧮 Table calculations are live! Nobody wants to create every permutation of a metric in your dbt project... Instead, you should be able to write metrics on the fly in the Lightdash UI. Introducing: table calculations 🎉 You can now build metrics in Lightdash, on the fly, from data in your query's table. Just click on the + button on your results table, pop in your custom SQL there, and voilà: table calculations. 🎭 You can now configure Lightdash to use any dbt target. Phew. Before this update, Lightdash could only be connected to your default dbt target (which, for most of us, was some dev target). Now, you have the option to configure Lightdash to use any dbt target of your choice in the lightdash.yml file (soon to be available in the one-click-deploy options as well). So, connecting Lightdash to your production environment is as easy as ever :) 🌩️ We've invited our first users to Lightdash Cloud We've sent out the first set of invites to the private Beta of Lightdash Cloud. With Lightdash Cloud, you get all the good stuff from the open source product, but it's hosted by Lightdash, has more team collaboration options and advanced support. Interested? 😏 You can sign up to the waitlist here and we'll get in contact with you to see if you'd be a good fit! 📤 out-of-the-box analytics with Lightdash We're building towards a future of out-of-the-box analytics with dbt + Lightdash: picture dbt packages, but with saved metrics, charts, and fill-in-the-blanks analyses. All available to you with the click of a button ✨. To start out, we've been playing around with some of the Fivetran <> dbt packages and added some Lightdash metrics into their dbt_hubspot package. So, with our updated package, you can get out-of-the-box dimensions and metrics for your Hubspot data in dbt. Sound like your cup of tea? 🍵 You can check out the updated package here. If you're interested in learning more about our plans for the "good future" of Lightdash, you can take a look at our product roadmap . We’re hiring - build the future of BI We have a bunch of open positions right now to join us on our mission to build the future of business intelligence on top of the modern data stack. We value being a small, high-performing team and operate 100% remote. If that gets you excited, we’d love to hear from you. Open roles: Full-stack Developer Frontend Engineer Product UI/UX designer Infrastructure / DevOps Hope you all have a great weekend! ❤️ Katie and the Lightdash team.

title: 2021/08/11 Roundup - What's an analyst's favourite sandwich? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1084 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: What's an analyst's favourite sandwich? ...an ELT 🥪 In the news Why Looker was and Lightdash is a big deal for BI - awesome write up from Gleb over at Datafold with a great take on the BI space and what lies ahead for us all. Latest product updates Here are just 3 of the most popular features we've released in the last 2 weeks: 🍰 Self-host Lightdash in 2 minutes Getting started with Lightdash is now a piece of cake. You create your own private instance of Lightdash, synced to your dbt Cloud account with just 1-click. It's Lightdash on your infrastructure with no effort. Have your cake and eat it. Check out the new tutorial to get started: 💾 Save charts and share them with your team Once you've crafted the perfect metric and visualisation, you can now save it and share it with your team. Naming stuff is hard!!! We had a lot of fun working with you (the community) to come up with the best name for a saved chart. 'Saved query' came out on top for technical folks but 'Saved chart' was voted the most accessible terminology across the whole org. Want to join in the shenanigans? It's all happening over in the #tools-lightdash channel in dbt's slack space: https://community.getdbt.com 🔒 Secure Lightdash deployments with email + password You can now secure your Lightdash app with an email and password. The first time you sign up for Lightdash you'll be asked to create a user and organisation. In the next week we'll be launching plenty of new features to manage your organisation (e.g. inviting your team mates to join you in Lightdash). We're hiring - build the future of BI We have a bunch of open positions right now to join us on our mission to build the future of business intelligence on top of the modern data stack. We value being a small, high-performing team and operate 100% remote. If that gets you excited, we'd love to hear from you. Open roles: Full-stack Developer Frontend Engineer Product UI/UX designer Infrastructure / DevOps You made it to the end! You are truly our biggest fan ❤️ Oliver and the Lightdash team.

title: 2021/07/15 Roundup - What's a ghost's favourite data type? · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1083 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: What's a ghost's favourite data type? Boo-lean 👻 Latest Product Updates ⚡ ☁️ You can now use Lightdash with dbt cloud! So many exciting new features have been shipped in the past two weeks! But, the top spot goes to (:drum: ): --> dbt cloud integration 🎉 You can now run Lightdash using a self-hosted dbt project or a dbt cloud project! Check out all of the details for how to setup your Lightdash project in our docs here. 📈 Sharing your charts is as easy as 1, 2, download .png You can now download + share your charts as JPEG , PNG , SVG or PDF . Because data is more fun with friends ;) 📅 Date filters have arrived! Want to find users with the same birthday as you? No problemo. In the newest release of Lightdash, we've added the ability to filter your data by date. Demo-ing our favourite dbt-specific features in Lightdash 😍 📹 Check out the feature demo here. We've made a quick video demo-ing our favourite dbt-specific features in Lightdash. Things like: how to connect a dbt cloud project to Lightdash seeing your models' lineage in the app viewing the source code for your dimensions/metrics right in Lightdash automatically syncing your dimension + metrics into your Lightdash project Watch the Loom video to see it all in action. 🔮 Planning the (near) future Last week, we spent some time working on our product roadmap and really thinking about where we want Lightdash to be in the next 3-6 months. In case you're as new to building product roadmaps as I was, here are two blog posts by Jens-Fabian Goetzmann that I found really useful in the process: In this one, they explain the difference between product vision, strategy and roadmap. I especially loved the "real life examples" they give of these concepts. 👌 In this one , they give you a "guide" and steps for actually building a product roadmap. You can follow along with our progress in GitHub here . That’s all folks! Thanks for checking out this week’s Roundup. Let us know if you have any questions or comments ❤️ Katie and the Lightdash team
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: 2021/07/02 Roundup - A SQL Query walks into a bar · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1082 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: 2/07/2021 Roundup - New features around data lineage, support for GCP for production deployment and more! Continued: A SQL query walks into a bar, walks up to two tables and asks “Can I join you?” Latest Product Updates ⚡️ The theme for the last 2 weeks has been focused on bringing a lot more context and visibility to the data you're using in Lightdash, with a bunch of features showing data lineage and how tables are joined together! We've also made it easier to deploy Lightdash to production - read on for more! Show lineage of tables in Lightdash You can now see the lineage of all your tables in Lightdash. Select "show lineage" on any table and you'll be shown a dependency graph of upstream and downstream tables. You can view the entire graph or filter it to only direct dependencies! See the source .yml file of any field When you click on an individual field or metric, you can now see its source dbt .yml file directly in Lightdash. You can also copy the file path if you want to open it in your own editor. Show SQL logic for table joins When tables are joined together in Lightdash, you can now see the SQL logic used to join these tables by clicking the join icon next to the table. Smaller but still exciting product updates: We added steps on how to deploy Lightdash to production with Cloud Run in Google Cloud Platform: Details here Much more friendly errors when dimensions and metrics are compiled on "dbt refresh" We now show you what version of Lightdash you're running and if an update is available A bunch of bugs and smaller UI tweaks - if you're interested in getting more into the weeds - check out our releases ! New blog post! Why we're building on dbt We wrote a blog post on why we're betting on dbt and why it's such a great tool for Lightdash to be synced with! Join the community! We have loads of exciting features we're working on, and you can follow them here or join the chat with us directly in our Discord . You can also follow and tweet us for all our latest updates ! That’s all folks! Thanks for checking out this week’s Roundup. Let us know if you have any questions or comments ❤️ Hamzah and the Lightdash team!
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: 2021/06/15 Roundup - What did fruit loops say when launching their product to compete with cheerios? 🤔 · lightdash/lightdash · Discussion #1081 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Toucan play that game What's a Roundup? 🤔 Hello hello! It's the very first Roundup (woo!) and we're excited about all of the things we have to share :) So let's start this one right and answer the question on everyone's mind: "What's this Roundup thing you're talking about?" Basically, Roundups are a bi-weekly update where we get to share what we've been up to and what we're excited about at Lightdash 🥳 So every other Tuesday, we'll post a new Roundup that you can find in the "Roundups" category . If you're more of an email kind of person, then you can sign up to our Newsletter (sign up is at the bottom of the page) and we'll send you an email with a summary of the Roundup and a link to the post each time it comes out. So now that the scene is set, let's get into some exciting updates! We released the first public version of Lightdash 🌩️ About two weeks ago, we decided that it was time to dip our toes in that sweet launch water and shared a public version of Lightdash on Show HN. We felt like we didn't want a full-blown launch just yet, so we picked a channel that we thought would get a bit of action, but nothing too crazy. Then...it got shared by Claire at dbt (thank you! ❤️ ), made it into Tristan's Data Science Roundup , and started popping up in the dbt community Slack 😁. We definitely weren't expecting this amount of buzz, and when we talked about it after, we all said the same thing in different ways: We were most excited about the fact that the community of people we were building this tool for were the people that were stoked about it. So ya, our "small" launch was great, but we still have a lot of room to grow and want to keep everyone super excited about what we're building! We launched our community forum 👯‍♂️ We set up Discourse early last week and have been thinking a lot about how we want to keep folks involved in building a community and a product that helps everyone answer their data questions. I actually thought so much about it, that I wrote a little blog post about why we want to build a community that you can check out here . We're planning to use Discourse as the primary tool to talk to our users/community and GitHub as the primary tool to plan our work/build the product. But, we're keepin' it hip and trendy, so we also have a Twitter account that you can follow. So follow us, star us, tweet at us - we'd love to hear from you :) That's all folks! Thanks for checking out this week's Roundup. Let us know if you have any questions or comments in the thread ❤️
author: TuringLovesDeathMetal

title: Evidence is now using GitHub Discussions! · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2328 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: We are moving across to GitHub Discussions for certain kinds of community engagement Discussions Feature Ideas New Data Source Requests Q&A: How do I...? Issues Bugs Documentation Requests or Errors Well scoped projects that are being worked on Why the move? The rationale for this move is twofold: Slack is a good short term forum, but is neither searchable nor well indexed, so it does not create reusable answers. We have a much larger number of Github Issues than our team has capacity to deal with. Discussions allows us to monitor for popular requests and engage on them. Wait, can I still get help on Slack? Yes

I chose it because I'm a SQL person and if I can avoid the big BI tools I will. || Feedback: Maybe this is possible, just browsed the tool quickly, but here are some thoughts: Great if there would be a CLI tool that we could run on internal networks, that gathers the data and build the reports, and then pushes these reports to the "hosting service". The hosting could be either self-hosted or your cloud. We'd be happy to use the cloud since the reports themselves won't be that "secret", however, we wouldn't be willing to give your cloud software access to our servers (data storage), for security reasons. The only "build target" right now seems to be a static HTML site with JS. It would be neat if it also supported e.g. "dumb html", so basically no interactivity in the widgets (maybe turning them into static SVGs or PNGs). For graph inspiration, take a look at https://metr || Great if there would be a CLI tool that we could run on internal networks, that gathers the data and build the reports, and then pushes these reports to the "hosting service". The hosting could be either self-hosted or your cloud. Would this be somewhat akin to Self-Hosted Runners ? || Probably closer to the CLI tools that are popular, for example https://docs.sentry.io/product/cli/ and https://docs.readme.com/main/docs/rdme . So basically, a push based mechanism where we can push updated data (based on the queries) to your cloud. The cloud would be the viewer, would handle access control for individual users, maybe inline comments and discussions on reports, and other user-to-user stuff. In the scenario I'm thinking of, the markdown source would be version controlled by us (that's the idea with Evidence, no?) and we'd run a job on a production node that would periodically run the queries and push the result to your system using a CLI tool (or your API). The benefit here is that if you get hacked, our database won't be. We'll pin your CLI tool with a SHA hash. Just like how we can integrate the Superset dashboard using their SDK. || There is. It's typically a custom setup using a JWT token. title: Evidence v40 static deploy on Vercel — all queries stuck on "Loading..." · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3292 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Problem: Evidence.dev v40 static deploy on Vercel — all queries stuck on "Loading..." Repo: dibeen-evidence (Evidence.dev v40, @evidence-dev/evidence@40.2.2 ) What works: npm run sources succeeds on Vercel — fetches data from Google Sheets via @evidence-dev/source-javascript , produces 13 parquet files npm run build ( evidence build ) succeeds with zero errors — DuckDB registers all tables at build time The built site deploys as static ( framework: null , outputDirectory: "build" ) All parquet files are accessible (HTTP 200) at their expected URLs manifest.json is accessible and lists all 13 tables DuckDB JS loader files load in the browser The WASM binary exists on the server (34MB, HTTP 200) What's broken: Every query on every page shows "Loading..." indefinitely — DuckDB WASM never executes queries client-side No errors in the browser console (or they're swallowed) This affects ALL pages, not just specific queries What we've tried: Fixed Vercel env var JSON parsing (literal \n characters breaking JSON.parse for the service account key) — this fixed the data pipeline but not the Loading issue Reverted all page content to last known working commit ( d5a647d ) — still Loading Added npm run sources to vercel.json buildCommand (was relying on build cache before) — sources run, data exists, still Loading Created a post-build script ( fix-manifest-paths.mjs ) to strip static/ prefix from manifest paths — tried both with and without it, still Loading Tried copying DuckDB WASM binaries to build/workers/ directory for locateFile fallback — still Loading Key observation: The dashboard used to work when evidence build ran alone (without a separate npm run sources step) and relied on Vercel's build cache for source data. The breakage started when we added the explicit npm run sources && npm run build pipeline. This suggests npm run sources (standalone) may produce data/manifest in a format that evidence build copies differently than when it runs sources internally. Specifically suspicious: npm run sources generates manifest paths with hash subdirectories: evidence_daily/1ebfdf18107557b7fc22c3439dc25b8b/evidence_daily.parquet Old working deploys may have had simpler paths without hash dirs The manifest paths include a static/ prefix that may or may not be expected by the client-side WASM engine What I think the issue is: The client-side DuckDB WASM engine can't find or initialize the parquet files despite them being on the server. Either the manifest format from standalone npm run sources is incompatible with how the static adapter serves files, or the WASM binary initialization is failing silently (perhaps a locateFile path mismatch for the .wasm binary relative to the worker). What would help: Has anyone successfully deployed Evidence v40 as a static site on Vercel with npm run sources as a separate build step? How does Evidence's client-side query engine resolve parquet file paths from the manifest? Does it expect/strip the static/ prefix? Is there a kno

title: Support of database-native spatial data in maps · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3290 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description I am working as an analytics engineer in the energy sector and we are using Postgres database as our data warehouse because we need rich spatial data manipulation support using the PostGIS extention. It would be great when Evidence could support the PostGIS "geometry" data type in the visualization in its maps (LineStrings, Polygons, Points and Multi-features). This way the geometry can be loaded directly from the database with the SQL statement without the extra steps of generating a geojson and storing it somewhere. What problem would this solve? No need to generate geojson and more support for all geometry types (like LineStrings) How should it work? Example for a point layer select id, 3 AS value, ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint( - 71 . 1043443253471 , 42 . 3150676015829 ), 4326 ) AS geom from my_spatial_table {% map %} {% point_layer geography="geom" data="spatial" area_id="id" value="value" /%} {% /map %}
author: SeppBerkner

title: Integration of PostGIS geometries · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3276 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description Our data warehouse is built in postgresql. As we are handling complex spatial it would be great to serve PostGIS geometries directly in Evidence, without the extra step of needing to generate and save geojson files from the database. This way we could serve a dashboard that creates choropleth maps of parcels or cadastral districts more easily. What problem would this solve? Native support of PostGIS geometries to remove steps of exporting the data unnecessarily to serve it to evidence. How should it work? My SQL query includes the PostGIS geometry column and I can use this column as a base for the spatial data of an AreaMap component

title: Clarify relationship between paid offering and open-source · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3177 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Congrats on the new studio, looks good! Will consider using it at my employer! :) A few questions and thoughts: Would be nice if you could clarify the relationship between this product and open-source. Looking at the commits, it looks like activity has basically stalled. Is it going to slowly die? Or are there parts of the open-source code still being used in the studio? Which ones? Basically it would be helpful to understand what parts you are monetizing, and what parts are open-source? Will you continue to write code in the open-source repo? If so, what parts? What type of use-cases will still be feasible under open-source only. Etc. Maybe also clarify if DuckDB is still being used internally in the studio, or if you've ditched it for some other database/storage layer? (image showing commits falling of a cliff early 2025) Not at all against the paid offerings. On the contrary, it's naive to think that you would just work indefinitely for free at some open-source code and nobody would be paying for it. But it would also help the community if you would clarify revenue plans, what's free, etc. For example (and this is just me guessing), it could look like this: Basic components, query language and UI will continue to live in open-source code. Free for others. SSO, user-sync, permissions etc is outside the open-source product. If you are self-hosting you need to manage this yourself. Chat and AI layer is outside open-source. We welcome bug fixes and improvements to widgets and charts. But please ask us first, because we won't accept PRs for niche features that we don't think most of our users will find useful. We will provide a basic self-hosting guide with 'known limitations' outlining what parts you will need to solve on your own. We encourage adoption of Evidence in other open-source projects, but will not accept PRs covering key features in our paid offerings, such as RBAC, SSO, … … … … (again, all of the above is just an example!!) @hughess @archiewood

title: Increase or remove sidebarDepth limit for deeply nested page structures · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3264 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description Currently, the sidebarDepth prop on EvidenceDefaultLayout has a maximum effective value of 3, limiting sidebar navigation to 3 levels of nested pages. For projects with more complex information architectures, this restriction forces compromises in URL structure or requires workarounds. Problem When organizing content into a logical hierarchy that exceeds 3 levels, the sidebar navigation stops displaying nested items beyond that depth. This creates a disconnect between the URL structure and the navigation UI. Users navigating to level 4+ pages have no sidebar visibility of where they are in the hierarchy, making navigation difficult. Proposed Solution Increase the default maximum from 3 to 6 or higher Or make it unlimited by default, letting users set a lower value if they want to collapse deep structures Add a "show more" expander for deep hierarchies that exceed the configured depth What problem would this solve? Navigation visibility for deep content hierarchies How should it work? Update core-components package: export let sidebarDepth = 3; 2 --> {#if secondLevelFile.children.length > 0 && sidebarDepth > 2} {#each secondLevelFile.children as thirdLevelFile} {/each} {/if}"> export let sidebarDepth = 3; 2 --> {#if secondLevelFile.children.length > 0 && sidebarDepth > 2} {#each secondLevelFile.children as thirdLevelFile} {/each} {/if} To support deeper nesting, Evidence would need to: Replace the hardcoded nested loops with a recursive component Pass sidebarDepth to control how deep the recursion goes This is a more significant architectural change than just increasing a number - the entire sidebar rendering logic would need refactoring. Ideal would be: ">
author: philsv

title: Option to load DuckDB WASM from CDN to reduce bundle size · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3259 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description Add an option to load DuckDB WASM files from jsDelivr CDN instead of bundling them into the build output. This would be controlled by an environment variable (e.g., EVIDENCE_DUCKDB_CDN=true ), keeping the current bundled behavior as the default. The @duckdb/duckdb-wasm library already supports CDN loading natively via getJsDelivrBundles() and selectBundle() , so this would leverage existing, officially supported functionality. What problem would this solve? When deploying Evidence sites to Cloudflare Pages, the build fails because DuckDB WASM files exceed Cloudflare's 25MB per-file limit. The WASM files bundled from @duckdb/duckdb-wasm are large enough to push deployments over this limit. Currently, Evidence bundles DuckDB WASM files directly from node_modules via Vite's ?url imports in @evidence-dev/universal-sql . There's no way to configure Evidence to load these files externally, which makes deployment to size-constrained platforms impossible. References: Cloudflare Pages limits DuckDB WASM CDN loading docs jsDelivr package How should it work? Set an environment variable to enable CDN loading: EVIDENCE_DUCKDB_CDN=true npm run build Under the hood, this would change packages/lib/universal-sql/src/client-duckdb/browser.js from the current bundled approach: const DUCKDB_CONFIG = useEh ? { mainModule : ( await import ( '@duckdb/duckdb-wasm/dist/duckdb-eh.wasm?url' ) ) . default , mainWorker : ( await import ( '@duckdb/duckdb-wasm/dist/duckdb-browser-eh.worker.js?worker' ) ) . default } : { mainModule : ( await import ( '@duckdb/duckdb-wasm/dist/duckdb-mvp.wasm?url' ) ) . default , mainWorker : ( await import ( '@duckdb/duckdb-wasm/dist/duckdb-browser-mvp.worker.js?worker' ) ) . default } ; To use the library's built-in CDN support: import { getJsDelivrBundles , selectBundle } from '@duckdb/duckdb-wasm' ; const JSDELIVR_BUNDLES = getJsDelivrBundles ( ) ; const bundle = await selectBundle ( JSDELIVR_BUNDLES ) ; const DUCKDB_CONFIG = { mainModule : bundle . mainModule , mainWorker : bundle . mainWorker } ; This keeps the default behavior unchanged while giving users an opt-in path for size-constrained deployments.

title: Support non-summable sankey diagrams · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3189 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description Currently, the sankey diagram component automatically sums input values for a given stage in order to calculate it's value. This is typical for additive diagrams, e.g. money flow. C([190]) B([90]) --> C([190]) " dir="auto"> graph TD A([100]) --> C([190]) B([90]) --> C([190]) Loading I would like to be able to use the the given value instead ( valueCol ). My use case is visualizing progress in a data pipeline: a bunch of files are flowing through some computation steps, and I would like to display the number of computed values for each step. For example, if step A has processed 100 files, step B has processed 90, and C has processed 85, then I would like to be able to see the following: C([85]) B([90]) --> C([85]) " dir="auto"> graph TD A([100]) --> C([85]) B([90]) --> C([85]) Loading What problem would this solve? Visualizing progress of a processing pipeline that is a DAG. How should it work? We could add a new parameter: valueMethod = sumOfInputs | fromValueCol # defaults to sumOfInputs
author: danielgafni
comment_excerpt: This is an interesting use case. However, I don't think Sankey diagrams are really designed to view data in this way. The first Sankey diagrams were used to present the flow of energy (a conserved quantity) in steam engines. Indeed, echarts (our underlying chart library for most 2D charts) does not support viewing data that is not additive / conserved. In your explanation above (which is very clear!) you are making use of Mermaid diagrams, which I suspect might be a better fit. We previously had a Mermaid diagram bundled with core Evidence charting library, but it added a large number of dependencies and JS for a single chart, so we decided to remove it. However, it would be relatively straightforwards to add your own custom mermaid chart based on mermaidJS https://github.com/evidence-dev/ || Thanks, I've considered using Mermaid. I'll try to make a custom component with some help from Claude -- hopefully it works for me, I was not sure how easy would it be to inject build-time data into it. What do you think of isolating this component into @evidence-dev/component-mermaid ? This would solve the dependency bundling issue (not sure if optional dependencies are a thing in JS). Edit: moved this question to #3190 (reply in thread)

title: Support for automatic synchronization between inputs and URL search parameters · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3175 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description To make filters persistent improves consistency of inter-page navigation, which enhances the overall user experience. Currently, Evidence has limited support for sidebar customization, and manually syncing inputs with the URL requires writing complex code. So, two features required: preserving search params on the page navigation synchronization between inputs and URL search parameters This feature request is a little bit more detailed than #1803 What problem would this solve? The official documentation offers opportunity to use filters: "> ``` sql products SELECT code, title FROM data . products ``` Dropdown data = { products } name = " product " value = " code " label = " title " defaultValue = " % " > DropdownOption value = " % " valueLabel = " All products " /> Dropdown > For many applications it is useful to be able to reflect the selected filter in the search of the url and vice versa. Currently to do that we need to write something like that: "> Dropdown data = { products } name = " product " value = " code " label = " title " defaultValue = { ( typeof window !== ' undefined ' ) && $page . url . searchParams . get ( ' product ' ) || ' % ' } > DropdownOption value = " % " valueLabel = " All products " /> Dropdown > `${value}` === NULL; const setFilterParam = (name, value) => { if (typeof window === 'undefined') return; const params = $page.url.searchParams; if (value === '%') { params.delete(name); } else { params.set(name, value); } const search = params.toString(); window.history.replaceState({}, '', `${window.location.pathname}${search ? '?' + search : ''}`); }; $: !isNull(inputs.product.value) && setFilterParam('product', inputs.product.value);"> const NULL = "(SELECT NULL WHERE 0 /* An Input has not been set */)" ; const isNull = value => ` ${ value } ` === NULL ; const setFilterParam = ( name , value ) => { if ( typeof window === 'undefined' ) return ; const params = $page . url . searchParams ; if ( value === '%' ) { params . delete ( name ) ; } else { params . set ( name , value ) ; } const search = params . toString ( ) ; window . history . replaceState ( { } , '' , ` ${ window . location . pathname } ${ search ? '?' + search : '' } ` ) ; } ; $: ! isNull ( inputs . product . value ) && setFilterParam ( 'product' , inputs . product . value ) ; And what is the worse -- It works only for the current page. The search is not preserved on the navigation between pages. The evidence doesn't allow to override Sidebar without breaking entire markup. How should it work? Add New Page Frontmatters Frontmatter Type Mandatory Description preserveSearch

title: Allow for "direct query" style queries · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2333 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature Description While the choice of pre-loading all the data together with DuckDB makes the experience of using evidence quite snappy, it also means that some types of dashboard are not viable - simply because they would require loading way too much data. It would be great if it were possible to allow some queries to hit the database directly. While it might seems undesirable, the number of OLAP databases that could handle this type of workload has exploded, and it would make the deployment easier and solution space (much) larger. Goal of Feature Expand the solution space from pre-aggregated data that fits easily into memory to the full set of (ad-hoc) questions one could ask from the data. For instance, drill downs per product, country, membership status, ... Current Solution / Workarounds Currently we need to have a ton of queries that precompute the drill downs we think are interesting, and disallow more than a single variable in these drill downs (ie sales per brand, sales per country, sales per channel) Alternatives Use evidence as a nice looking (very basic) email report, and use another tool for the deep-dives. Super unsatisfying, because it would require multiple tools again (and those tools can usually also send emails)

title: Query Performance · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3101 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description While talking with @ItsMeBrianD about the new Pivot Table component, he raised some concerns about the performance of queries and using pagination to (ideally) make them more performant. The way Query is currently written, pagination would create many queries and might not destroy/free memory when they are unused. This could make the Pivot Table sluggish. These ideas would benefit Pivot Table performance in particular, but also the whole app in general. They are not a pre-requisite to the Pivot Table, but the performance may be poor without these. Time will tell. Mutable queries Don't return new query from .where() , .limit() , etc - limits object creation/destruction Violates a lot of assumptions Components that accept a query shouldn't modify the query because it could be used elsewhere (maybe fixed with a .copy() method?) Currently, query state never goes backwards (e.g. once a query is done , it can never be in progress again) Make sure we are loading data lazily length and describe queries should happen on Query creation data query should wait until the value of the query is used This would have a big impact on long chains of query methods ( .where().skip().limit() ) - avoid executing middle queries Better cleanup of unused queries to reduce memory overhead Wouldn't do this and mutable queries, they're mutually exclusive What problem would this solve? No response How should it work? No response

author: PowerPlop
comment_excerpt: As context, pre universal-SQL support, this is how all Evidence queries worked (ran against data warehouse). The problem this had, among others, is that you could only connect to a single database, and there was no support for client side interactivity. We plan to add support for querying external data sources in the future, though this will likely be via DuckDB rather than using the data warehouse directly. (For example, external parquet files) In the meantime, you can do aggregation in the source layer if you need to, and just query the aggregates. || Thanks for the clarification. title: Improvements for Input Syntax · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #3038 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Existing situtation There are a number of rough edges with current input syntax Initial states are hard or sometimes impossible to set correctly It may not be possible to know the values for a query before page load as they may depends on other inputs with unknown states Selecting all requires inlining all the query results for multiselect which can have perf implications causes from table where column_name in ('a','b','c',...'z') Initial state ( select null where 0 /*An input has not been set*/ ) causes a query to not run on initial page load SQL select syntax is different for single select and multi select Multi where column_name IN vs Single where column_name = Wide differences in implementation structure of input keys inputs.my_input vs inputs.my_input.value vs inputs.my_input.end Inputs statements are verbose to write where column_name = '${inputs.my_input.value}' Meta: SQL is not ergonomic for writing dynamic filter statements Possible Improvements Standardise the use of IN with all select based components (docs only change) Multiple: IN ${} Single: ='${}' -> IN '(${})' Replace all bare inputs with thing → ('thing') Allows usage of IN ${} throughout all dropdown components in combination with 1. Allow a select all behaviour in multi dropdowns that when there is no selection, uses the column name where column_name IN ${inputs.myinput.value} -> where column_name IN column_name we can use the column_name provided in the input if specified in the value prop Possibly, make 3 configurable so that it can also return where column_name = '' to allow a none selection that works in all cases Collapse syntax so you don't have to explicitly call inputs. (and possibly "values") {inputs.picker_name.value} → {picker_name.value} {picker_name.value} → {picker_name} Remove the confusing need for $ in queries IN ${inputs.picker_name.value} → IN {inputs.picker_name.value}
author: archiewood
comment_excerpt: The Dimension Grid returns true when nothing is selected, and inlines the entire IN statement. For example, instead of ( 'Microscope','Running Shoes' ) the Dimension Grid returns ( "item" in ( 'Microscope','Running Shoes' ) Making the other inputs act like the Dimension Grid is a possible improvement. I like how the Dimension Grid returns multiple filters from a single source table (one for each column) too. Would be neat to have the same syntax but for a group of dropdown inputs.

author: PowerPlop
comment_excerpt: Currently the best model to do RLS style access controls is by managing different deployments for different users. Since each deployment is simply a static site, this is a relatively lightweight solution. You can combine multiple deployments with build time variables to filter the data per user, and that way create totally sandboxed data for each user. Evidence Cloud has features to make the managements of these deployments straightforwards. || So if we would have 5 sales manager representing each a district and they should not be allowed to see each other figures, you would deploy 5 times and manage access through different sub-domains etc.? || either sub domains or sub routes with separate storage behind them || Thanks for the swift reply!

That's similar to how Observable Framework data loaders work. You write data loaders in any language , and Observable Framework will capture whatever's written to stdout, even console.log() statements. The output can be in any of the 17 supported formats (JSON, CSV, etc.) in Observable, but that also requires you to specify the file format when you use the file. For Evidence, I'd think it's just simpler to have every output file standardized as parquet. || I've just published a javascript connector - this means anything you can run in javascript you can load into evidence without a new connector. It will handle all the conversion to parquet for you. All you need is to export a variable called data https://github.com/archiewood/evidence-connector-js sources/pokemon/pokedex.js let url = 'https://pokeapi.co/api/v2/pokemon/' const response = await fetch ( url ) const json = await response . json ( ) const data = json . results export { data } index.md ``` sql pokemon select * from pokemon . pokedex ``` || That was quick! Thanks Archie. Will give it a try. || Did you try this out?
