title: Deploy Evidence to non-root path / folder · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2329 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Evidence Maintainer Edit Non-root paths are not supported, but we are keeping this issue up to date as we frequently get asked about this. You may want to consider the following alternative options: deploying to a subdomain : subdomain.yourwebsite.com embedding Evidence inside your app : using an iframe or similar on your desired subfolder It is possible we will support non-root paths in the future, but it is not on the near term roadmap. Below are some workarounds though they are likely to be fragile. Feature Description I would like to override sveltekit's base URI and others settings like outDir when I run evidence build . The documentation of sveltekit suggests to use svelte.config.js, Goal of Feature I would like to deploy a static evidence's build on Github Pages or something equivalent. Github Pages requires the urls to be relative or following the URI of the repository, so I need to set the sveltekig --base parameter. But it is not suffisant because the URIs in the Evidence.dev html (ex /favicon.ico ) are all hard coded to point to the root "/". Current Solution The current source code in cli.js does not allow to override svelte.config.js , it overrides the svelte.config.js everytime we run evidence build So my current solution is using unix's find to track all urls mv -R ./build ./public find ./public/* -type f -not -path '*.ico' -exec sed -i '' "s,=\"/,=\"/my_github_uri/,g" {} +
author: BenJoyenConseil
comment_excerpt: @BenJoyenConseil - I came here looking for help on the same. My issue logged below seems to describe the same issue you describe above. Published Evidence site is getting 404 on subpages meltano/jaffle-shop-template#6 Are you still using the above workaround? I tried on my side but it didn't seem to work. || A few possible ways to implement this: An option in settings + env vars to set the root path A cli flag Allow an override of the svelte.config (not preferred, similar to treatment of layout) Finished: successful deployment on github pages || Hi all, We took a look at supporting this, see: #764 , and are not going to move ahead for the time being. While exposing the base path configuration option is straightforward, supporting it across our entire builtin component library, and maintaining it is too much of a headache relative to the other things we'd like to be focusing on at the moment. We'll likely revisit this, since there are use cases for this beyond just GH pages, but in the meantime netlify and vercel are both great deployment targets for public projects. || And I guess you can just map your repo website to a subdomain, while still being hosted at GitHub Pages? Then it only requires you to setup a subdomain, which requires 1 additional file in the repo and some dns changes. Your pages are then hosted at the subdomain without a repo directory in front. || For what its worth, I was able to get this working by doing the following, but it is a cat and mouse game each time we upgrade Evidence/svelte/vite so maybe not worth it. Turning off vite preload so that static assets aren't loaded from the wrong absolute path (I had to do this by manually modifying node_modules/@evidence-dev/evidence/template/vite.config.js during build, which is ugly, but would be straightforward if it were possible to adjust vite.config.js directly) override +layout.js to grab data from a data from a relative path instead of absolute || @jdimmerman this is cool Out of interest, did you have issues with breadcrumbs, table of contents, sidebar etc not knowing about the changes to the base path? || That's a good question... We are not using any of those features or routing in general - all one page with tabs we styled to be on the left - so my comment may be entirely worthless to others || Ben's script did not work for me on Ubuntu, this worked (on a very trivial example, this will definitely break for something more complex): BASE_PATH= " /public " find public/ -type f \( -name " *.js " -o -name " *.html " \) -exec sed -i " s,href= \" /,href= \" $BASE_PATH /,g " {} + find public/ -type f \( -name " *.js " -o -name " *.html " \) -exec sed -i " s,src= \" /,src= \" $BASE_PATH /,g " {} + echo " URL replacement complete. " I hope this helps anyone. Good luck. || Ben's script did not work for me on Ubuntu, this worked (on a very trivial example, this will definitely break for something more complex): BASE_PATH= " /public " find public/ -type f \( -name " *.js " -o -name " *.html " \) -exec sed -i " s,href= \" /,href= \" $BASE_PATH /,g " {} + find public/ -type f \( -name " *.js " -o -name " *.html " \) -exec sed -i " s,src= \" /,src= \" $BASE_PATH /,g " {} + echo " URL replacement complete. " I hope this helps anyone. Good luck. It is probably because of the different implementations of the Sed tool between mac and GNU world (the -i option with empty quotes is for mac implem) || You're absolutely correct! Although I have to say it's unfortunate we even have to use this "hack"-around 😶‍🌫️

title: Warning Dots in DataTable · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2334 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature Description Warning dots that appear in a row when a threshold is exceeded or criteria is met. Nice to have would be on hover, showing the reason the specific item exceeded the threshold. Challenging part of this will be how to structure the criteria. Even using a simple threshold is not straightforward because we would need to know if above or below triggers the warning. I think the simplest way to implement this would be: Require user to include a boolean column in their query which evaluates the rule For cell-level: Column accepts a warning prop, which is a reference to a column containing the boolean info When true, a circle is added next to the data within the column For row-level: DataTable also accepts a warning prop When true, a circle is added on the far left or right of the row If this logic works for warning dots, it can also be applied to other forms of conditional formatting - e.g., highlight a full row. Cell-Level Warning Dot Row-Level Warning Dot

title: Metrics and/or Data Documentation Visible to Developers and End Users · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2656 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Description From this slack thread The TL;DR is that developers and end users can see how data and SQL powering the Evidence app is defined. Like Looker's ? info icon that showed SQL and user defined descriptions on hover in the interface. What problem would this solve? It would save a lot of time, effort, and accuracy if developers and end users could see how data is defined from the interface instead of either not knowing or having to ask around. For example, if in the data warehouse sql some label bucketing column values is defined, or the SQL powering an Evidence chart has a where clause, the end user is not going to know what goes into each bucket or chart filter unless the developer explains so in the Evidence markdown (or the end user is savvy enough to view all the sql queries which may not be realistic). However, any hard coding of column explanations would mean the Evidence app is effectively outdated as soon as it is deployed, so ideally there is something more 'programmatic' in this sense. How should it work? It would be great to be able to hover over a column in Evidence and see some kind of lineage or data explanation be presented. For example if a column is defined with a case statement and the sql powering it is something like when column_a in ('x', 'y', 'z') then 'label_i' else 'label_j' end then the end user should be able to view that quickly without having to go digging through the view SQL queries themselves. I find myself wanting something like dare I say a LookML-like semantic layer...or at the very least the ability to write a piece of logic and reuse it like a dbt macro i.e. define a 'data_labelled' constant in a way that can write the sql and also the markdown using the same piece of reusable code like a variable. Then the sql and the markdown will always be linked.

title: Waterfall Chart · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2372 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: A waterfall chart is great for showing decomposition of changes. I've had a start at creating one (which I will open in a draft PR for those who are interested in taking a look). Considerations for implementation Axis labels are important on these charts and will often be long strings - the chart needs to be able to handle multi-line labels without cutting off characters or breaking words apart I think it probably needs "breaking" behaviour by word, followed by either truncation ( ... ) or expansion of the chart size This will also need to work well responsively and on mobile We don't currently have multi-line axis labels in our other charts - this will need some thinking to make sure the chart area is the right size in all cases. Currently, adding multi-line axis labels shrinks the main chart area The initial attempt I've made requires a specific data format: category - category to use on x-axis value - change to show on chart total - boolean - should this be treated as a total/subtotal on the chart The SQL required to get data into this format can require a lot of work. We might want to offer other alternatives for the structure of the data getting passed in I don't think this would change the implementation within echarts - we could just add a layer of logic above this to get it into the format echarts requires Data labels should be included - positioning these may be difficult I haven't thought about tooltips yet. I'm thinking they will be straightforward though - probably a config override sent from Waterfall to Chart Subtotals - the logic for generating the chart series will need to be updated to accomodate subtotals - currently, having a total in the middle of the data throws off the cumulative sum We may want to offer sorting - e.g, sort by magnitude of change. This shouldn't be hard to do with this data format since we can easily identify the totals and exclude them from sorting In a lot of cases, people need to start the y-axis at a higher number than 0 to make the changes visible enough on a waterfall chart We could offer a yMin prop to allow someone to set it manually I could also imagine some dynamic logic to check the change range (max of cumulative sum - min of cumulative sum) as a % of axis range and adjust the yMin so that the change range takes up something like 50% of the vertical chart area
author: hughess
comment_excerpt: The SQL required to get data into this format can require a lot of work. We might want to offer other alternatives for the structure of the data getting passed in I think including a sample SQL query in the documentation would go a long way. It is indeed fairly complex but you can simplify it massively with clever windowing (LAG / LEAD or cumulative sums). I think having a clean example of the data model on this one is key because it always takes me like an hour in power BI to get these right. || @matsonj Does the below interpret what you are saying correctly? There are a few common uses for waterfalls, for example Revenue bridge (by category, geography etc) Volume value bridge (revenue start, impact of price, impact of volume, impact of mix, residual, revenue end) We should write an example of how to wrangle a standard table format in order to do a waterfall of the above types eg Assuming your columns are this: date , revenue , category Then use this example query to get into waterfall format Assuming your columns are this date , item , price , quantity , [total revenue] Then use this other query And as a follow up question: what are your most typical waterfall use cases (assuming not covered by the above two)? || @archiewood yeah, I think those are the core cases. I think the core questions I usually run into that usually are answered best with waterfalls are the following: Why did revenue change this month vs last in percentage and absolute $? (i.e. change in revenue by type - recurring, churn, growth, etc) Why did profitability change this month vs last month in percentage and absolute $? (i.e. contribution margin by product line) Could go down the line on any GL line item too, but those are the two that seem the most frequent in my exp. || Is Waterfall still on the Backlog, or is it closed for now? 😊 || Still on the backlog, would definitely welcome contributions! || Awesome! Unfortunately, I don't think I have the abilities to contribute... 😅

title: On demand refresh for near-realtime use cases · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2380 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature Description A "refresh" button in the Web UI, which would queue up an on-demand refresh. Goal of Feature This could help with use cases that require near-realtime data, by allowing Evidence to get the latest data without having a window into the backend via SSH, and without acruing query costs of refreshing a full set of data every n minutes. This might be used in combination with a daily refresh or n-times daily. Variations / Implementation options Where the button might be: This could be put on a special "Admin view" page. This could be at the top of each rendered page, hidden under an exapandable "dataset on this page" widget. This could be part of the expandable data section per each chart. What the button might do: Could refresh one selected dataset and only the active page. Could refresh one selected dataset and all impacted pages of the site. Could refresh all the datasets on the active page. Could refresh all the datasets on the site. Atomic or global: This on-demand refresh could leave other pages/visuals alone, versus also auto-updating other pages or other visuals on the current page, versus refreshing the site anywhere that dataset is rendered. Bonus: For transparency reasons, it becomes even more helpful to have a place on the page that renders "last updated on..." per dataset, and/or for the page overall. (Harder to do if not all visuals on the page have the same refresh time.) Current Solution Today I think the best solution is to refresh every n minutes. Alternative Ideas Alternatives include: Refreshing every n minutes on a schedule. (Noted above.) Define certain queries "on-demand" whenever the page loads. Not mutually exclusive with this option, but the refresh-on-demand approach balances flexibility along with render performance; whereas an on-demand query would add significant lag. Automatically queuing data refreshes when a page reaches a certain staleness threshold, along with a notification to the user that the refresh has been queued. This saves the user from needing to push a button, and is cheaper than running every n minutes, but is more complex overall, and may be better implemented on top of on-demand refreshes, as a future iteration. Examples / Screenshots Not the same, because this is the Admin view, but Power BI has a feature like this in their web UI: Show/Hide Are you interested in contributing this feature? In theory 'yes', but I don't have capacity as of now, and I am not yet well-versed enough on the underlying codebase to do so efficiently. Related Is there any mechanism to control the update of the chart? #534
author: aaronsteers
comment_excerpt: @aaronsteers thanks for opening, always appreciate your input 🙏🏼 A couple of bits of context for anyone who is not intimately familiar with how Evidence works. How Evidence currently works Evidence has two "modes" Development (dev): the experience that a developer uses when building a project Build for production (prod): the experience that an end user of evidence sees online Unlike a lot of tools, these modes operate differently with respect to connecting to data Development There is a live connection to the data warehouse Queries only run for the page you are currently accessing in the browser Queries run live against the database Queries data is cached for x minutes (currently 15mins), unless the query is changed Production Evidence's build process generates a static site All queries in || Thanks, @archiewood ! Yeah, great context - thank you. 🙏 I especially like this part, and would like to keep it for production applications: All updates to the site are behind version control, and changes to the database will never break the live site Given that, I think any kind of on-demand refresh or near-realtime features should ideally fail back safely to the last-valid dataset. And very handy for transparency if something like a warning icon with tooltip message (or similar) could appear if any post-build refresh attempts have failed, such as an on-demand refresh request. || @aaronsteers something like this is becoming feasible as we make progress on #768 and #973 . It'll be down the road though. You can play with a prototype version here: https://github.com/evidence-dev/template/tree/next The above supports multiple simultaneous data sources, which can be updated outside of the process of building the front-end, which does open up a whole new set of possibilities, including near realtime, different schedules for different sources, incremental source refreshes, and snapshotting source data. When we bring this to main, the initial versions of the new build/ refresh data will just be for dev mode, but you can track progress on that here: #988 || @mcrascal Any updates for this scenario now that Universal SQL is launched? Can dynamically updating Parquet files (or directory) be reflected in charts without any manual steps to update?

title: Improve the queries directory to better fit w/ USQL · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2406 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature Description The details of the queries/ directory are a bit of a hold-over from pre USQL. The way they work now is kind of incongruent w/ the mental model of USQL, and takes you down a path of writing really non-performant pages. Queries in the queries directory help you accomplish two things: Cross source: Light data modelling across all your sources (e.g. joining the results of a postgres query to a snowflake query) Building light pipelines (e.g. a small dag of queries) Quick refactor target for queries you're repeating in pages. Copy/paste, keep the same syntax. Even if you would eventually want to full refactor the query into your source. Today, there are two issues "Importing" queries in front matter, and having them rendered on the page encourages users to put very large unaggregated queries on the page, which is a performance drag You need to have a query on the page in order to use another one which references it. Here's how I think they should work: Frontmatter references should not be required Queries in queries/ should only be able to reference sources and other queries in the directory. When we're compiling a chained query on a page, we should first check on the page for a query of that name, then check the queries directory, and swap in the compiled results from the queries directory.

title: custom timeout for `mssql` connector · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2414 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature Description Currently, it seems impossible to set a custom timeout when connecting through the mssql connector. When I have a query running more than 15000 ms, I get the following response when running npm run sources : The mssql connector uses npm package mssql under the hood . According to the npm docs , this package allows for setting custom requestTimeout and connectionTimeout , which both default to 15000 ms. Looking at the mssql connector source code, it seems there would be some opportunity for injecting custom requestTimeout and connectionTimeout somewhere here . I would suggest you just allow for a single timeout option, which steers both mssql timeouts under the hood, while still defaulting to 15000 ms. Goal of Feature Allow mssql users to run a query taking longer than 15s. Current Solution / Workarounds This is breaking my current process. Alternatives Not sure for now. I've already optimised the heck out of my queries. Other connectors offer larger timeouts already. Take a look at BigQuery 's 3,600,000 ms timeout .

title: Auto Materialization of source tables · evidence-dev/evidence · Discussion #2410 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Feature Description Ability to get tables directly from source, more concisely, for SQL based databases connection.yaml name: prod type: snowflake options: ... tables: - orders - reviews or even name: prod type: snowflake options: ... tables: - * Goal of Feature Faster DX when prototyping The ability to more quickly specify sources in SQL connectors faster visibility of the columns in your source database tables Current Solution / Workarounds sources/orders.sql select * from orders sources/reviews.sql select * from review Considerations This would automatically do a select * which may be undesirable Especially tables - *
author: archiewood
comment_excerpt: Should likely limit the content also or this is a potential to blow up huge tables, or cost a lot. Eg, imagine the costs of running this on a bigquery db with billions of rows. Could be quite expensive queries. If you use datagrip or some other editor they usually limit to some fixed size (500 rows) for you automatically behind the scenes when you do stuff. You could have a default limit, that is override-able tables: - orders - limit: 500 - reviews - limit: 0 #unlimited rows || Agree that this could be dangerous and we should think about how not to destroy people's DBs However also automatic (unspecified) limits can also be confusing and lead to errors in analysis (Easy to miss a dropped row if there were 505 rows in the table, and you excluded the last 5) An alternative would be to refuse to run tables above x rows (eg x=100,000) unless the user specifies limit=0 (or rows=all or similar), and throw a warning in this case? || Although now I think about it, that would require a count(*) operation on the table which is sometimes (depending on DBMS) an expensive operation

title: Using Plausible + StatScribe for daily analytics habit · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6231 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: honestly been using Plausible for like 8 months now and it's been such a relief compared to what I was doing before. the simplicity is actually the point, you know? I don't have to spend 45 minutes every morning deciphering sessions and events. anyway, wanted to mention something I've been doing that's worked really well for my workflow. I built a small tool called StatScribe that sits on top of Plausible data, and I've found it solves a specific problem I kept running into. the issue was: I'd log into Plausible, see the numbers, and then just... stare at them. like "okay bounce rate is 58%, visitors are up 23%, but what does that actually mean for what I should do today?" I'd spend another 20 minutes cross-checking metrics trying to figure out if something was actually good or if I should worry about it. StatScribe basically automates that thinking part. it pulls your Plausible data and generates a quick morning briefing that's just like "here's what mattered, here's what you might want to look at today." takes 2 minutes to read, doesn't try to be a replacement dashboard (Plausible already does that great), just gives you the interpretation layer. obviously not a prerequisite for Plausible or anything—plenty of people probably don't need that extra step. but if you find yourself doing a lot of manual analysis on top of Plausible's data, might be worth checking out. anyway, huge props to the Plausible team for keeping things simple. that's half the battle right there. https://www.statscribe.app

title: Anyone using Plausible for reddit ad campaigns? · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6230 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I'm new to the domain of visitor tracking and I'm thinking of creating an ad campaign that will run on reddit. Hence, I wondered how it's possible to track users coming from reddit. Below is what I learned and am sharing, hence I'd be glad if someone experienced could review. I learned that reddit has a pixel; i.e. a small js script with a personalized id for impression tracking, which comes with a long legal document , which made me doubt using it. Reddit lists Third-party measurement providers , which doesn't list Plausible. Since it's an impression(i.e. page view) tracker, it can already be covered by Plausible: As far as I understood, by using utm_ parameters and by creating several different ad campaigns that has different & specific subreddits, one could track how many users could come from each subreddit. But that's manual and reddit already exposes more details. If someone have previously successfully started using plausible along with reddit ad campaigns, could you please share your experience?

title: "No need for cookie banners" might be incorrect · plausible/analytics · Discussion #1963 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: I am primarily posting this because I recently wrote a blog post on the topic (update: I've written a new version of the blog post after new EU guidelines came out) , and felt it only fair that you got the opportunity to comment. I am not a lawyer, so this is based on my best understanding of the legislation; whether you want to react on it or not is up to you. The landing page for Plausible advertises "No need for cookie banners or GDPR consent" in a heading. While I believe the latter part is correct, I would argue that cookie banners are still required, even when using a privacy-aware system like Plausible. The ePrivacy Directive simply doesn't have an exemption for anonymized data. I dive deeper into this in the blog post linked above, but the short version is that not using cookies, anonymizing the collected data, and not storing any data on the user's device, simply isn't enough to be exempt from the ePrivacy Directive: Not using cookies/not storing data Although a common misconception, helped by the unofficial "cookie law" misnomer, this actually isn't a requirement. As put by Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive: Member States shall ensure that the storing of information, or the gaining of access to information already stored, in the terminal equipment of a subscriber or user is only allowed on condition that the subscriber or user concerned has given his or her consent, having been provided with clear and comprehensive information, in accordance with Directive 95/46/EC, inter alia, about the purposes of the processing. Any form of storing or gaining access to stored information requires informed consent. Even something as simple as reading the User-Agent is covered, as argued in the working group's opinion on fingerprinting : Use of the words “stored or accessed” indicates that the storage and access do not need to occur within the same communication and do not need to be performed by the same party. Information that is stored by one party (including information stored by the user or device manufacturer) which is later accessed by another party is therefore within the scope of Article 5(3). [...] The consent requirement also applies when a read-only value is accessed (e.g. requesting the MAC address of a network interface via the OS API). Anonymization Unfortunately doesn't exempt from the ePrivacy Directive. As summarized by the working party's 2014 opinion on anonymization : 2.2.1. Lawfulness of the Anonymisation Process First, anonymisation is a technique applied to personal data in order to achieve irreversible de-identification. Therefore, the starting assumption is that the personal data must have been collected and processed in compliance with the applicable legislation on the retention of data in an identifiable format. In my opinion, this means that even users of privacy-aware analytics like Plausible and Fathom must present their users with a "cookie banner" in order to get informed consent. Again, I am not a lawyer so I this
author: birjj
comment_excerpt: I've seen this argument before, but the following needs to be considered. No data about the user is stored or processed. Consent to anonymous data being collected is when you land on the page. It would be unreasonable to expect otherwise. The data collected is to used to understand, optimise and enhance experience of the website / service. Therefore regardless of 'your' consent I am going to collect the minimum. Why? Otherwise I'd struggle to predict traffic or general usage. I'd argue this is required for the site to function correctly for all users. That statement is not unreasonable. Data collected has absolutely no value to anyone else and could never be used to target that user. There are plenty of projects operating on this assumption. This is badly written by the EU (no shock!) and  || I think your points are largely addressed by the legislation: No data about the user is stored or processed. Data about the user is accessed and processed - specifically the algorithm creates a fingerprint from things like the Referer header, the User-Agent, the screen size and a GeoIP lookup of the IP address. The simple act of accessing these things is covered by the ePrivacy Directive as argued above, and anonymization is considered a step of post-processing (that's actually required by the Directive as soon as the data is no longer relevant, but that's another conversation) Consent to anonymous data being collected is when you land on the page. It would be unreasonable to expect otherwise. It is very explicit in the legislation that you must get informed , explicit consent. Arguing tha || hi @birjj ! if you're unsure about our approach, it's best to consult with your legal team. we provide detailed information on how Plausible is built to help you comply with the different privacy regulations in documents such as the data policy and the DPA . by looking at this info, your lawyer can help you decide whether our service allows you to fulfill the legal requirements that apply to you. thanks! || Can we at least agree to stop quoting bullshit machines LLM:s as some sort of truth? || Following for sake of my home-grown DIY solution, intent to not even blur truth but only track what's indisputable. Are you able to identify users between visits? Then you are tracking users. Plausible & Goat do salted, expiring, pseudonymize. Which I think you're still disputing. But if it was just sesssionStorage (just multiple pages in a single browsing session; not return visits) this would be fine, right? (I'm only using sessionStorage in my DIY). Do you collect information about those users? Then GDPR applies. I don't think this is true. There are concepts of anonymous standard date; legitimate interest data; fingerprinting, etc. So there are thresholds in what's valid non-user traffic data on the left; fingerprinted user data on the right; and a grey zone in the middle that's very m || Related: umami-software/umami#2929 (comment) || To my understanding, if you're tracking users, GDPR applies. Are you able to identify users between visits? Then you are tracking users. Do you collect information about those users? Then GDPR applies. That's not true for the GDPR ; while GDPR is fairly complex (and I don't have a good complete overview of it), it primarily legislates storage and processing of personally identifiable information (essentially any information that might conceivably be used to conclude something about the user). While it's easy to erronously conclude that that means any data at all, strong anonymization solves even the letter of the law: Note Anonymous data, on the other hand, cannot be associated to specific individuals. Once data is truly anonymous and individuals are no longer identifiable, the data will n || @birjj with the ePR withdrawn , do you know if this upcoming "digital package" will replace everything else? || Great and challenging discussion? The CNIL (French data protection authority) is keeping a list of analytics softwares allowed to track without consent . It would be great if Plausible.io submits a valuation request , to have the CNIL opinion about the issue! || If I understand that page correctly, it only talks about consent required by the GDPR. The issue discussed here is that the ePrivacy Directive requires consent to even read data from the end-user device, or to use traffic data for non-traffic purposes. It is possible to be in compliance with GDPR (and be listed on that page), but not in compliance with the ePrivacy Directive (and therefore still require informed consent). While the GDPR is too complicated for me to confidently argue about, I would assume that Plausible could be argued to be GDPR compliant. It just isn't (in my opinion) ePrivacy Directive-compliant. Very interesting link though, thanks for sharing it!

title: Regression on datacenter traffic filter? · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6210 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, since a couple of weeks I get plenty of fake traffic reported in Plausible. My website's visitors are mainly from European country, and and use the site during daytime in Europe. Since 19th of March, I get plenty of visits from Mexico, China, Singapore, the US, at any time of the day and night. Checking my nginx logs, I was able to confirm that these visits are all from datacenter IP addresses (Tencent, Huawei). There are so many different IP addresses it's not really conceivable to block them all. Coincidentally, I upgraded plausible from 3.0.1 to 3.2.0 on the 14th of March - 5 days before the problem appeared. So I'm wondering: did I possibly mess something something during the upgrade? Or did Plausible's datacenter traffic filtering stop working? Something else? Related discussion or another apparently hit by the same spam traffic problem recently: #6206
author: mef
comment_excerpt: Thanks for the report @mef . I just wanted to clarify that our advanced bot and datacenter traffic filtering is a cloud-only capability and has never been part of the self-hosted release. On plausible.io we actively monitor traffic, maintain blocklists and filter out bots and datacenter traffic on an ongoing basis. This isn't something we can do on self-hosted instances. You can learn more about this and other differences between the cloud and self-hosted here . The 3.2.0 upgrade didn't change anything related to this, so the timing is coincidental.

title: Spammers galore · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6206 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, We are getting a LOT of fake traffic it seems. I've been blocking stuff in Cloudflare with rules, but there are still so many getting through. Clearly spammers with fake UA's, as there is no way someone would hit 2 and 3 pages with 1 second between. I've been blocking hosting AS blocks like https://ipinfo.io/AS47583 (of which an IP was probing the site). But surely there is a better way? Cheers Andy
author: youradds
comment_excerpt: I realised that the spam was coming from api requests sent with fake referers. So I added this in to my nginx config: location = /api/event { if ($http_origin = "") { return 403; } if ($http_origin !~* "^https://(mysite\.com|www\.mysite\.com)$") { return 403; } proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8000; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for; } It seems to be doing the job now. I thought I would post it here in case it helps anyone in the future Cheers Andy

author: Arrekin
comment_excerpt: The broken avatar with NS_ERROR_CONNECTION_REFUSED on /avatar/xxxx calls is likely caused by the avatar service endpoint changing during the upgrade chain from v2.0 to v3.2. A few things to check: 1. Verify your BASE_URL is correct The NS_ERROR_CONNECTION_REFUSED error means the browser tried to connect to something that actively refused the connection. Look at the full URL in the browser's Network tab — if the avatar request is pointing to localhost or 127.0.0.1 instead of your actual domain, your BASE_URL env var is misconfigured. # Make sure this matches your actual deployment URL including protocol BASE_URL=https://plausible.yourdomain.com 2. Check if the avatar route is proxied correctly In v3.x, avatars may be served through a different path. If you're using a reverse proxy (Nginx/Ca

title: help with a workaround for data-domain deprecation · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6118 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: in the new script, there is no longer a data-domain variable which is problematic for me as my app is deployed into multiple domain (ie: dev, test, prod) since it is a frontend react app, the code is compiled and i used to be able to build a different version of my app for each domain easily by specificying something like data-domain="%VITE_DATA_DOMAIN%" it becomes somewhat cumbersome to have to save (in this case) three version of the .js file and then reference each for each build any ideas on a reasonable alternative?
author: airedwin
comment_excerpt: The data-domain attribute was removed in newer versions of the Plausible script because the script is now tied to a specific site via the script URL itself (each site gets its own script endpoint). For your multi-environment React setup, here are a few approaches: Option 1: Use separate Plausible sites per environment Register dev.yourapp.com , test.yourapp.com , and yourapp.com as separate sites in Plausible. The newer script auto-detects the domain from window.location.hostname , so it will route to the correct site automatically — no data-domain needed. This is probably the cleanest approach if you use different domains per environment. Option 2: Dynamic script injection via env variable Instead of a static script tag, inject it dynamically based on the build environment: ; }"> // Plaus

body_or_excerpt: Hi all! When hosting Plausible CE for multiple products, I use DNS aliases so each product gets its own tracking subdomain (e.g. plausible.foo.com , plausible.bar.com ) all pointing to the same instance. BASE_URL is set to one of them. The problem: the generated tracking script hardcodes BASE_URL as the /api/event endpoint. E.g. given BASE_URL is plausible.bar.com , a site configured using plausible.foo.com to load the script still sends events to plausible.bar.com . This breaks Content Security Policy on sites that only allowlist their own tracking domain in connect-src. Current workaround: add the BASE_URL origin to connect-src on every site, which leaks the relationship between products. Is there an existing way to handle this that I'm missing? If not, a suggestion would be to allow setting a per-site tracking URL (e.g. a "Site URL" field in site settings) that the generated script uses for its event endpoint instead of BASE_URL. Thanks for building Plausible CE: loving it so far!
author: andreasscherman
comment_excerpt: @andreasscherman Thanks for your kind words! The endpoint for the autogenerated tracking script is a configurable value. You can set the endpoint in plausible.init call, example below. window.plausible = window.plausible || function () { (plausible.q = plausible.q || []).push(arguments) }, plausible.init = plausible.init || function (i) { plausible.o = i || {} }; plausible.init({ endpoint: "https://plausible.example.com/api/event" }) demo "> > html lang =" en " > head > script async src =" https://plausible.example.com/js/pa-....js " > script > script > window . plausible = window . plausible || function ( ) { ( plausible . q = plausible . q || [ ] ) . push ( arguments ) } , plausible . init = plausible . init || function ( i ) { plausible . o = i || { } } ; plausible . init ( { endpoint : || Thank you, I completely missed that. ✅

title: v3.2.0 · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6026 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: This release adds " Limit to segment " feature for shared links fixes a bug requesting tracker scripts in v3.1.0 ( issue 5901 ) fixes Clickhouse low-memory settings that weren't actually applied ( Fix low resources settings for Clickhouse community-edition#265 ) ...contains a few other fixes and changes, full details below Upgrade Checkout the branch plausible/community-edition:v3.2.0 . Needed because Clickhouse profile settings in the community-edition repository have changed. console $ git fetch origin v3.2.0:v3.2.0 $ git checkout v3.2.0 Re-apply any local changes to tracked files in the repository. If all configuration changes were in .env and compose.override.yml , this step isn't necessary. console $ docker compose up -d This will boot up the new version of the app. Changelog Added A visitor percentage breakdown is now shown on all reports, both on the dashboard and in the detailed breakdown Shared links can now be limited to a particular segment of the data Changed Segment filters are visible to anyone who can view the dashboard with that segment applied, including personal segments on public dashboards Fixed To make internal stats API requests for password-protected shared links, shared link auth cookie must be set in the requests Fixed issue with site guests in Editor role and team members in Editor role not being able to change the domain of site Fixed direct dashboard links that use legacy dashboard filters containing URL encoded special characters (e.g. character ê in the legacy filter ?page=%C3%AA ) Fix bug with tracker script config cache that made requests for certain cached scripts give error 500 This discussion was created from the release v3.2.0 .

title: Clarify or split “Views per visit” when using event-level filters · plausible/analytics · Discussion #6057 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: First of all, thanks for the explanation on issue #5804 — I understand and agree that the current calculation of “Views per visit” is internally consistent and intentionally session-based. That said, I think there is still a UX and product clarity issue worth discussing. In the Plausible dashboard, users see three metrics side by side: Total Pageviews Total Visits Views per visit In the unfiltered view, “Views per visit” effectively equals Pageviews / Visits, which strongly sets the user expectation. When an event-level filter is applied (e.g. a custom property), the first two metrics reflect the filtered data, but “Views per visit” silently changes its semantic meaning: Pageviews: filtered events Visits: sessions with at least one matching event Views per visit: average total pages of the entire session, not the filtered events This makes the metric no longer derivable from the two numbers shown next to it, which is very confusing for most users. The natural mental model (“just divide the two”) suddenly breaks, with no explanation in the UI. I fully agree that the current definition is useful as a session-based engagement metric. The problem is that: the metric name doesn’t reflect that nuance the dashboard doesn’t indicate that a different semantic is being applied the behavior is especially confusing for event-level properties, which are increasingly common Possible ways to reduce confusion without changing the underlying calculation: Add a tooltip or info text explaining that “Views per visit” always uses full session pageviews Rename the metric when event-level filters are active (e.g. “Avg pages per matching session”) Eventually expose both interpretations as separate metrics I think this would help align Plausible’s internal logic with user expectations, especially for less technical users.

title: Only allow admin user to add new websites · plausible/analytics · Discussion #1906 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Currently, with publicly exposed self-hosting Plausible instances, where registration and login are not disabled, it's possible to register new user accounts, add any websites, and use the dashboard without any limits. It would be nice to have a setting like USERS_CAN_ADD_WEBSITES . When this setting is set to FALSE (I suggest this should be the default), anyone can still register, but only the ADMIN_USER can add new websites (other users can be invited to become Admins or Viewers of these sites, but can never become Owners). title: Use Winkel-Tripel projection for countries map · plausible/analytics · Discussion #174 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: "The Mercator projection currently used in the countries map does not accurately represent the geographic size of countries. Since land mass has some correleation to population of a country, this projection overstates the potential audience size of each country. See https://thetruesize.com/ for a fun interactive example of this distortion. The Winkel-Tripel is the projection preferred by the National Geographic Society. From a mathematical perspective, it may be the most correctly balanced with respect to the various types of distortion introduced by map projections: area, isotropy (local scaling), flexion (bending), and skewness (lopsidedness). The Goode homolosine projection would also be a better choice than Mercator, but it splits Greenland in two." ( imported from the old roadmap )
author: metmarkosaric
comment_excerpt: It looks like you are using DataMaps . The default projection is equirectangular , a more scale-accurate projection than Mercator. Removing this property should immediately improve the map: analytics/assets/js/dashboard/stats/countries.js Line 73 in b930b82 || Hoping to see this change implemented.

title: v3.1.0 · plausible/analytics · Discussion #5884 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: This release Introduces the new dynamic and more configurable tracking snippet and script (legacy scripts and snippets will still continue to work). See this doc for more details Supports a new optional property on events: interactive . See this doc for more details. Revamps dark theme ...other fixes and improvements listed below Upgrade Update the image used for plausible compose.yml plausible: - image: ghcr.io/plausible/community-edition:v3.0.1 + image: ghcr.io/plausible/community-edition:v3.1.0 and restart the containers console $ docker compose up -d This will boot up the new version of the app. Changelog Added Custom events can now be marked as non-interactive in events API and tracker script: events marked as non-interactive are not counted towards bounce rate Ability to leave team via Team Settings > Leave Team Stats APIv2 now supports include.trim_relative_date_range - this option allows trimming empty values after current time for day , month and year date_range values Properties are now included in full site exports done via Site Settings > Imports & Exports Google Search Console integration settings: properties can be dynamically sought Weekly/monthly e-mail reports now contain top goal conversions Newly created sites are offered a new dynamic tracking script and snippet that's specific to the site Old sites that go to "Review installation" flow are offered the new script and snippet, along with a migration guide from legacy snippets, legacy snippets continue to function as before The new tracker script allows configuring transformRequest function to change event payloads before they're sent The new tracker script allows configuring customProperties function hook to derive custom props for events on the fly The new tracker script supports tracking form submissions if enabled The new tracker script automatically updates to respect site domain if it's changed in "Change domain" flow The new tracker script automatically updates to respect the following configuration options available in "New site" flows and "Review installation" flows: whether to track outbound links, file downloads, form submissions The new tracker script allows overriding almost all options by changing the snippet on the website, with the function plausible.init({ ...your overrides... }) - this can be unique page-by-page A new @plausible-analytics/tracker ESM module is available on NPM - it has near-identical configuration API and identical tracking logic as the script and it receives bugfixes and updates concurrently with the new tracker script Ability to enforce enabling 2FA by all team members Removed Changed A session is now marked as a bounce if it has less than 2 pageviews and no interactive custom events All dropmenus on dashboard are navigable with Tab (used to be a mix between tab and arrow keys), and no two dropmenus can be open at once on the dashboard Special path-based events like "404" don't need event.props.path to be explicitly defined when tracking: it

Thanks for taking a look at it and having a solution with the restart is good too! Thanks for the awesome release btw 🚀 || Finally managed to reproduce. Steps to reproduce Add new site Verify installation Go to Settings > General Click Review installation Change one of the checkbox options, like File Downloads Click Verify installation Go to script URL Expected Script served with updated options injected Actual Internal server error Issue The problem is with the CE calling Plausible.Cache.broadcast_put -> Plausible.Cache.Adapter.put -> ConCache.dirty_put when persisting the updated tracker config. That function doesn't overwrite the value at cache key, but rather makes the value into a list and appends the new value to the list. When trying to get the cached script, instead of the script string, we unexpectedly receive that list, and there's an internal server error. Processes that are supposed to refresh rece || Working on a solution now. On the plus side, that new release also will have some new features in it. || Sounds awesome! || The fix is included in the version v3.2.0-rc.0 .

title: v3.2.0-rc.0 · plausible/analytics · Discussion #5973 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: This release adds " Limit to segment " feature for shared links fixes a bug requesting tracker scripts in v3.1.0 ( issue 5901 ) ...contains a few other fixes and changes, full details below Upgrade Update the image used for plausible compose.yml plausible: - image: ghcr.io/plausible/community-edition:v3.1.0 + image: ghcr.io/plausible/community-edition:v3.2.0-rc.0 and restart the containers console $ docker compose up -d This will boot up the new version of the app. Changelog Added A visitor percentage breakdown is now shown on all reports, both on the dashboard and in the detailed breakdown Shared links can now be limited to a particular segment of the data Changed Segment filters are visible to anyone who can view the dashboard with that segment applied, including personal segments on public dashboards Fixed To make internal stats API requests for password-protected shared links, shared link auth cookie must be set in the requests Fixed issue with site guests in Editor role and team members in Editor role not being able to change the domain of site Fixed direct dashboard links that use legacy dashboard filters containing URL encoded special characters (e.g. character ê in the legacy filter ?page=%C3%AA ) Fix bug with tracker script config cache that made requests for certain cached scripts give error 500 This discussion was created from the release v3.2.0-rc.0 .

author: mlissner

title: [Guide] The easiest way to move plausible instance to new VPS · plausible/analytics · Discussion #4389 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi all, I decided to sum up all I did to move my existing plausible instance to new VPS. It turned out to be the easiest way comparing to others I found around the internet. It took me less then 10 minutes to migrate all and getting it up and running, but I do not have many data there. I recently moved to Hetzner's cloud arm64 VPS, which is blazing fast, cheap a powerful comparing to my previous amd64 hosting platforms. Even the plausible website is much more responsive then it was before. I could notice it when browsing stats. It took me multiple tries before I got it working so it is is crucial to do it exactly in this order :-) Requirements new VPS ready with docker installed Detailed step-by-step 1. Clone plausible dir on new VPS Clone the dir with clickhouse folders etc and most importantly docker-compose.yml and plausible-conf.env from old VPS to the new one using rsync. This is the command: sudo rsync -azvP -e ' ssh -p {port number} -i /home/user/.ssh/root-private-key ' ' root@[IPv6 address] ' :/srv/plausible /srv/ 2. Get it all up and running on new VPS Enter the cloned directory cd /srv/plausible and run sudo docker compose up -d , wait until all the images are downloaded and containers started, running healthy etc. 3. Stop all containers on new VPS Now stop all containers: sudo docker compose stop plausible plausible_db-1 plausible_events_db-1 4. Delete all volumes on new VPS Delete all the volumes which were created in the step 2 (few minutes ago) sudo docker volume ls DRIVER VOLUME NAME local plausible_db-data local plausible_event-data local plausible_event-logs sudo docker volume rm plausible_db-data plausible_event-data plausible_event-logs 5. Stop all containers on old VPS Now move to old VPS and stop all the containers: sudo docker compose stop plausible plausible_db-1 plausible_events_db-1 6. Clone volumes from old VPS to new VPS Move to the new VPS and check your docker volumes directory. I chose docker to use /srv/docker/ , yours can be the default /var/lib/docker/ , so adjust the URLs and clone volumes from the old VPS, again using rsync: sudo rsync -azvP -e ' ssh -p {port number} -i /home/user/.ssh/root-private-key ' ' root@[IPv6 address] ' :/srv/docker/volumes/plausible_db-data /srv/docker/volumes/ sudo rsync -azvP -e ' ssh -p {port number} -i /home/user/.ssh/root-private-key ' ' root@[IPv6 address] ' :/srv/docker/volumes/plausible_event-data /srv/docker/volumes/ sudo rsync -azvP -e ' ssh -p {port number} -i /home/user/.ssh/root-private-key ' ' root@[IPv6 address] ' :/srv/docker/volumes/plausible_event-logs /srv/docker/volumes/ 7. Get it all up and running again on new VPS Enter the cloned directory cd /srv/plausible and run sudo docker compose up -d , wait until all the containers are running. 8. Fix the webserver config and DNS Depending on the setup you have, you have to change your webserver config and DNS settings. For myself it was only to clone Caddyfile and to replace CNAME target value with new VPS URL. 9. Test eve

comment_excerpt: 👋 @myazaki Thank you for the guide! I would warn however to not delete the volumes (step 4) that contain the events data until you are completely, absolutely, 💯 sure everything was migrated successfully. || You are deleting volumes on new VPS (the one you are about to transfer all the data to), created by install script few minutes ago so there shouldn't be anything important there. Anyway, thank you for comment, I still did some tweaking to make it hopefully more clear :-) || For posterity: I recently wrote a blog post about this topic (I use another approach – I don't copy the volumes, but dumps of the Postgres / ClickHouse tables). title: "FATAL: role "root" does not exist" error every 30 seconds in the plausible_db container · plausible/analytics · Discussion #4370 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hello! I'm new to Plausible so I apologize if this is a stupid or irrelevant question. Since I installed from the example docker compose file (and successfully seeing page views show up) I've been getting a consistent error every 30 seconds on the database container: `2024-07-17 16:48:46.936 UTC [563] FATAL: role "root" does not exist 2024-07-17 16:49:16.975 UTC [572] FATAL: role "root" does not exist` etc I'm not sure if it's a functionality issue, but wanted to make sure it wasn't a sign of an installation issue or anything. Thanks
author: MisterC925
comment_excerpt: 👋 @MisterC925 This looks like someone trying to connect to PostgreSQL using root user, e.g. using psql without -U flag. Either way, it's not a problem if everything else is working as expected. || Thanks. The only containers on the network are the three the compose file builds, there's no outside access (and I'm not trying to manually access anything) enabled. I'll ignore it for now but might ask again if I notice it impacting functionality. Thanks! || I think it's the healthcheck which tries to connect as root: healthcheck : test : pg_isready start_interval : 2s start_period : 1m I replaced it with: healthcheck : test : " pg_isready -U postgres " start_interval : 2s start_period : 1m and the error is gone. || 👋 @drlogout Right, it was the healthcheck! It should be correct since ~v2.1.2: https://github.com/plausible/community-edition/blob/v2.1.2/compose.yml#L10 || Dang It was the healthcheck hahahaha overlooked that one!

title: Plausible crashing on startup · plausible/analytics · Discussion #4973 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I was previously running Plausible CE adapter from the docker-compose file to a Kubernetes deployment and had that working just by running the same container image with the right volume mounts and environment variables set. I'm now trying to do the same thing but as a .container podman/systemd/Quadlet definition, as follows: Environment=CLICKHOUSE_DATABASE_URL=http://host.containers.internal:8123/plausible_events_db Environment=DATABASE_URL=postgres://plausible: @host.containers.internal:5432/plausible Environment=HTTP_PORT=8000 [Service] Restart=always [Install] WantedBy=default.target"> [Unit] Description=Plausible [Container] ContainerName=plausible Image=ghcr.io/plausible/community-edition:v2.1.4 Label=io.containers.autoupdate=local Network=slirp4netns:allow_host_loopback=true Volume=/opt/plausible:/var/lib/plausible Ulimit=nofile=65535:65535 PublishPort=8016:8000 Exec=sh -c "/entrypoint.sh db createdb && /entrypoint.sh db migrate && /entrypoint.sh run" Environment=TMPDIR=/var/lib/plausible/tmp Environment=BASE_URL=https://my.site.example.com Environment=SECRET_KEY_BASE= Environment=CLICKHOUSE_DATABASE_URL=http://host.containers.internal:8123/plausible_events_db Environment=DATABASE_URL=postgres://plausible: @host.containers.internal:5432/plausible Environment=HTTP_PORT=8000 [Service] Restart=always [Install] WantedBy=default.target I've managed to get through db creation and migrations but ./entrypoint.sh run is failing and crashing without outputting any logs. When it runs I just get this: Loading plausible.. Starting dependencies.. Starting repos.. Plausible.Repo database already exists Plausible.IngestRepo database already exists Creation of Db successful! Loading plausible.. Starting dependencies.. Starting repos.. Runtime terminating during boot (terminating) Crash dump is being written to: erl_crash.dump... Any ideas what I may be missing here?
author: yaakov-h
comment_excerpt: 👋 @yaakov-h Could it be a permission issue on /opt/plauisble ? Similar to plausible/community-edition#206 || Thanks, that pointed me in the right direction. It was a permissions issue, but chown 999 didn't work as podman has a user and group mapping system when using rootless containers. I had to: chmod 777 to get Plausible to start Check to see what uid and gid Plausible was actually running as (uid was 100998 for example). A quick way to do this is ps -o user $(pidof beam.smp) chmod 755 to get permissions back to normal chown to the actual host uid and gid || Network=slirp4netns:allow_host_loopback=true Side note: Support for Slirp4nets will be removed in Podman 6.0 (if things goes according to plan) See Podman 6.0 design document . Solution: Use Pasta instead. To achieve something like allow_host_loopback=true with pasta, see Outbound TCP/UDP connections to the host's localhost

title: Prevent invitation emails from ending up as spam · plausible/analytics · Discussion #3225 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: When a user is invited to Plausible, the e-mail they receive is likely to end up in the spam folder, as the "score" associated with this e-mail is low. (test done with https://www.mail-tester.com/ ) It would be nice if these recommendations were taken into account
author: Striffly
comment_excerpt: 👋 @Striffly Thank you for the recommendations! I wonder how low the final score is. The website suggests that "a score below -5 is considered spam," but the numbers on your screenshot don't add up to that. Also, are you using the default SMTP adapter? An easy way to up the score would be to switch to a hosted mail service like Mailgun, etc. AFAIK, the SMTP adapter's goal is mostly ease of use, not avoiding the spam folder. || The sent email does seem to contain both the message-id and date headers (check the "Source" section), not sure why they are shown as missing by spamassasin. || @ruslandoga : The sent email does seem to contain both the message-id and date headers (check the "Source" section), not sure why they are shown as missing by spamassasin. How exactly do you send emails? I'm setting SMTP_* env variables for the docker container and all emails I checked neither contain Date nor Message-Id headers. Delivered-To: receiver@example.org Received: from mail.ud09.udmedia.de ([127.0.0.1]) by mail.ud09.udmedia.de with LMTP id KDJvNoUFQmVyQD8ALK0PDQ (envelope-from ) for ; Wed, 01 Nov 2023 09:00:05 +0100 Delivered-To: receiver@example.org Received: (qmail 4145525 invoked from network); 1 Nov 2023 09:00:05 +0100 Received: by mail.ud09.udmedia.de with ESMTPSA (TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 encrypted, authenticated); 1 Nov 2023 09:00:05 +0100 X-UD-Smtp-Session: receiver@b/PDqBI || I can confirm the missing message-id and Date headers in the sent messages. Plausible hosted in the cloud and it is configured to use mailserver on premises via the port 587 authentication. Amavis is marking the mails sent by plausible with bad-header warning. || @madanyang 👋 What's a mailserver? If it's a relay, then it should add the proper headers if they are missing before sending the mail to the end users. That's what mail does, for example. || It is not a relay. Plausible authenticates to the mail server and sends the mail. In your example bytemark/smtp is generating them. In my case plausible is connecting to the mail server directly. Date should be the responsiblity of mail client not the server. In this case plausible acting as a client does not create a Date field. || The current library that's used for SMTP is supposed to only be used with relays. The next release might add another client that would allow direct sending. || Quoting from RFC 5322 : The only required header fields are the origination date field and the originator address field(s). All other header fields are syntactically optional. More information is contained in the table following this definition. Quoting from RFC 5322 : The origination date field consists of the field name "Date" followed by a date-time specification. orig-date = "Date:" date-time CRLF The origination date specifies the date and time at which the creator of the message indicated that the message was complete and ready to enter the mail delivery system. For instance, this might be the time that a user pushes the "send" or "submit" button in an application program. In any case, it is specifically not intended to convey the time that the message is actually transported, but ra || Hello, I have the same issue with mails sent from plausible. They are marked as spam because of a missing-messageid and missing-date. Has someone found a solution in the meantime? title: ClickHouse with multiple replicas and shards · plausible/analytics · Discussion #5439 · GitHub

I have found a discussion that highlights that Plausible does not support clusters. I am quite new with ClickHouse: does that actually mean that Plausible can only run with ClickHouse if it has one shard and one replica? Just to understand if it is a limitation of Plausible or if I am doing something wrong with my setup. Thanks in advance.

title: Migrate to User-Agent Client Hints · plausible/analytics · Discussion #3420 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Major browsers are freezing the User-Agent, meaning they'll have less fidelity and it won't be sufficient for identifying the browser. Over time, the user-agent will be deprecated. The suggested replacement is to use Client Hints, which is an API that replaces the User-Agent. The server can request client hints by using a header, and there's also a JS API to request the client hints. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/User-Agent_Client_Hints_API Plausible should migrate from using User-Agent to instead use client hints. Plausible should consider using client hints in browsers that support them, falling back to User Agent in others.
author: Daniel15
comment_excerpt: Experimental: This is an experimental technology Check the Browser compatibility table carefully before using this in production. This is far from should migrate. It's something to keep an eye out for going forward, probably in a couple of years once the browser support is there. It's not even gonna leave experimental any time soon probably. || You're right. I should have said that Plausible should consider using client hints in browsers that support them, falling back to User Agent in others. Google Chrome has already frozen the user agent and migrated to user agent hints, and accessing the user agent in JS logs a deprecation notice. One of the benefits of user agent client hints is that you don't need complicated code to parse the user-agent, since each piece of information is a separate field. || FWIW, UA Client Hints seem to be the only reliable way to distinguish "Edge on Xbox" from "Edge on Windows" — the browser has the same user-agent string on different platforms || That's likely going to be the case for more hardware in the future. AFAIK neither Chrome nor Firefox are planning on adding new User-Agent strings.

title: New library: Expo client for Plausible Analytics (expo-plausible) · plausible/analytics · Discussion #5721 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi all, I’ve been working on a library called expo-plausible , which brings Plausible Analytics integration to Expo (React Native) apps . The package is lightweight, TypeScript-first, and follows the official Plausible Events API . It provides a simple provider + hook model, optional offline queueing, batching, and consent helpers. 🔧 Features PlausibleProvider + useTrackEvent hook Offline queueing with automatic retry Optional batching of events Consent helpers ( enableTracking , disableTracking , etc.) Nested providers with config inheritance TypeScript support 📦 Install pnpm add expo-plausible ⚡ Quick start trackEvent({ name: 'Signup', url: 'https://example.com/signup' })} />; } export default function App() { return ( ); }"> import { PlausibleProvider , useTrackEvent } from 'expo-plausible' ; function Screen ( ) { const trackEvent = useTrackEvent ( ) ; return Button title = "Signup" onPress = { ( ) => trackEvent ( { name : 'Signup' , url : 'https://example.com/signup' } ) } /> ; } export default function App ( ) { return ( PlausibleProvider config = { { domain : 'your-domain.com' } } > Screen /> PlausibleProvider > ) ; } 📖 Docs & API reference Full documentation: [link to your repo/docs] API essentials: PlausibleProvider({ config }) with domain , apiHost , batch , enableOfflineQueue useTrackEvent() → send events with name , url , props flushQueue() , enableTracking() , disableTracking() , isTrackingEnabled() 🙋 Why I’m posting here To share this library with others in the Plausible community who might be looking for an Expo/React Native integration. To ask maintainers whether you’d consider linking it in the official docs under “third-party libraries.” To get early feedback and confirm if there are any API best practices or recommendations I should align with. 🔗 Repo 👉 GitHub repo link Thanks, — Umidjon @ukutaht @aerosol @zoldar @RobertJoonas @macobo @ruslandoga

I think the error was thrown when the service was started. || Automatic database migrations happen between each release. That's what db migrate in compose.yml's plausible.command is for :) || Should we go through each versions when upgrading for these migrations to happen, or can we jump from 2.0.0 to 2.1.4? || Either way should be fine. However, without your logs ( #5012 (comment) ) I can't say what the problem is right now. || Today we started to go through all the versions to see where the problem could be. body_or_excerpt: Hi, This isn't strictly a Plausible question, but as it's a vital step in making Plausible useful in its self-hosted form, I wondered if anyone had an example of the config for HAProxy > nginx which forwards the client IP to my web server when they hit HAProxy (so Plausible & geo-ip can work with it. I've tried variations around the following, but my server only ever sees the HAProxy private-IP: # HTTPS Frontend frontend https-in bind *:443 ssl crt /etc/letsencrypt/live/pem/ option forwardfor header X-Real-IP http-request set-header X-Real-IP %[src] Thanks
author: steveharman
comment_excerpt: I know this is old, but i just had this issue with missing geodata in plausible using HAProxy. All i needed was option forwardfor inside frontend https-in , nothing else :) || Tacking on some more info for others that might search this - for HAProxy on pfsense. On each Backend - under Advanced Settings - Backend passthrough - add option forwardfor

title: Clickhouse eating up all my server's resources · plausible/analytics · Discussion #5535 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Past Issues Searched I have searched open and closed issues to make sure that the bug has not yet been reported Issue is a Bug Report This is a bug report and not a feature request, nor asking for self-hosted support Using official Plausible Cloud hosting or self-hosting? Plausible Cloud from plausible.io Describe the bug Clickhouse is constantly accessing it's config.xml and just breaking. I'm not entirely sure what's happening but there are multiple instances of it and its bringing down my server. If I turn off my plausable docker contianer, it doesn't happen anymore and I get back 50-80% of all my server resources. So I know it's plausable. Expected behavior I don't think it should be using THIS much memory/cpu usage. Screenshots Environment - OS: Ubuntu - Browser: - Browser Version:
author: DeveloperLewis
comment_excerpt: 👋 @DeveloperLewis ClickHouse isn't accessing config.xml, it's just the command that was used to start the process :) || 👋 @DeveloperLewis ClickHouse isn't accessing config.xml, it's just the command that was used to start the process :) I see! I guess this is more of a clickhouse issue then? || Maybe there are more knobs to tune to try to make ClickHouse consume less in Plausible CE. Prev discussion: plausible/community-edition#185 || I have exactly the same problem. CPU is running at 95-100%, RAM is at about 40% - which is fine. @DeveloperLewis Have you solved it somehow? || I've been doing quite a lot of testing and think I have a solution. I've no idea why ClickHouse uses CPU when nothing is happening in the database however, this was my config for ClickHouse and my CPU usage has been lowered significantly. false 1 2 1024 268435456 2 2 2 "> false 1 2 1024 268435456 2 2 2 Right now im still testing it so I don't know the effects on the system but currently with a rough ~25 concurrent connections it seems to be working as expected. The following config is a mashup of ClickHouse docs + third party (forgot the website where i got it from) which is apparently optimized for RAM usage too. This also works and keeps my CPU low. false 268435456 67108864 16777216 2000 64 32 0.50 0 1 2 1024 268435456 2 2 2 1 round_robin 1 1 2 8 1 1 0 0 0 0 1 1000 600 0 0 1073741824 107

title: Building on top of Plausible for an alternative dashboard · plausible/analytics · Discussion #5548 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: Hi, I'm Tjalf, an Information Technology and Design student from Germany. For my bachelor's thesis, I am designing an alternative web analytics dashboard that applies gamification concepts. The dashboard aims to make users feel more connected to their website visitors. The rough concept is to visualise each unique visitor as an avatar with a randomly generated name on an interactive map, displaying their session information and several other details, to create a playful experience, such as greeting avatars when opening the dashboard. While my thesis focuses on creating a UX and gamification concept, as a starting web developer, I am motivated to create a working prototype built on top of Plausible that could be used by others. However, due to my lack of experience, I need suggestions on the project structure: The biggest issue is that I need a version of the session entries to make this work, and as far as my research goes, this is not possible with the plausible API. That brings me to two approaches I can think of. One is to build a separate service to add to the Plausible CE Docker Compose that connects directly to the events_db via ORM. The other is to work directly with the Plausible Analytics source code and extend the React part as well as the Elixir backend API. In my expectation, the ideal implementation just has a toggle button at the top area of each dashboard to switch between the classic Plausible dashboard and the gamified one, which might not be possible with the first approach. The second approach feels a lot more challenging, though, building on the existing Elixir codebase and might lack support of future releases of Plausible. I am really happy for any suggestions or recommendations on how to proceed with my ideas :)
author: Trullfax

title: Stats API - period=realtime · plausible/analytics · Discussion #1744 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: I would like to get a breakdown of my top pages in real time. This is currently impossible, as the shortest time period is one day and the realtime endpoint only gives the number of realtime visitors. Would be nice if I could set the period parameter value to realtime . Then the API could interpret that as the last 5 minutes, and it would feel pretty much like real time. (feedback via email)
author: RobertJoonas
comment_excerpt: In addition, I think adding the filters parameter to the realtime endpoint would be huge for us. Trying to add current visitors on a product page but can only return realtime views for the entire site. || Plausible has a non-public API for this which it uses itself. A bit frustrating that it's not part of the supported APIs. The request is: https:// /api/stats/ca-17.com/pages?period=realtime&date=2023-11-17&filters=%7B%7D&with_imported=true&limit=100&page=1&detailed=false It's not ideal because you'll will need to pass your dashboard cookie header. Btw, the "detailed" parameter seems to have no effect. The result looks like this: [ { "bounce_rate" : null , "name" : " /map " , "pageviews" : 10 , "time_on_page" : 32 , "visitors" : 7 } ] || Bump. This works (using a Cookie header instead of API key). But why? Why isn't this part of the documented API?

title: If many individuals are using identical devices in one building, they count as one visitor. · plausible/analytics · Discussion #1525 · GitHub
body_or_excerpt: The current system for identifying unique visitors doesn't work in a setting like a school where the students are given centrally administered devices and share an IP address. According to the documentation, the unique identifier is: hash(daily_salt + website_domain + ip_address + user_agent) If many devices are connected to one wifi network they may share an IP-address. If they are all the same device and centrally updated, they likely have the same user agent. I don't have a solution that preserves the privacy focus of plausible but if anyone else has an idea I would appreciate it. My website is mostly used in schools, many of which are using class sets of chromebooks so I can't see how many people are using the site at each location.
author: whscienceteacher
comment_excerpt: hi @whscienceteacher ! yeah, that's correct. if multiple of your visitors have the exact same IP address and use exact same device then our unique visitors number would see them as the same visitor. we don't have a good solution for that at the moment unfortunately. introducing a first party cookie could work for this use case but it's not something we offer at this stage. || Users with the same IP and device still register separate views. Would it be possible to give the option to see the top pages, location, and devices sections of the website by pageviews instead of visitors? || at the moment it's not possible to replace visitors with pageviews in our UI. if you click on the "details" view in the Top Pages report you can see the pageviews there already. if you filter by any page or any location or any device, you can see the total pageview number in the top graph too. we have this data in our stats API too which may be a more convenient way to pull them. you can also get it so you can sort it etc using our CSV export functionality || No workaround for this yet? || Sorry for upvoting an old topic, but I would like this as well. Perhaps a method to trigger a "the current visit is over, a new unique visit starts" event? Any tips or tricks welcome.

author: jkoop
comment_excerpt: Hello @jkoop If you haven't already solved this issue, I dealt with something similiar hosting my app on AWS. In my case, I realized that following the official documentation, I was configuring tha app to listen locally: services: plausible: ports: - 127.0.0.1:8000:${HTTP_PORT} In this case, the app is not bound to the network interface. I needed to change it to 0.0.0.0:8000.

author: realulim
comment_excerpt: The max_server_memory_usage serves as a soft limit, and ClickHouse only utilizes Linux cgroup memory limits to enforce it when configured. You possess a BackgroundSchedulePool consisting of 512 threads, which is excessively high for a server with 8 GB of RAM. || All my settings are default as they come with the Helm chart. The values.yml file of the Helm chart has no entries for either max_server_memory_usage or BackgroundSchedulePool. Do you know where these values are configured? Maybe some config file on the actual container?
